issue_title: --slurp doesn't work since 2.3.5
issue_body:
If I try to use `--slurp` with yq, it gets incorrectly translated into `--slurpfile`. I am using Python 3.5.3.

```
> touch 2.yaml 3.yaml
> sudo pip3 install yq==2.3.4
> echo | yq -e --slurp '.[0] == .[1]' - 2.yaml 3.yaml
true
> sudo pip3 install yq==2.6.0
> echo | yq -e --slurp '.[0] == .[1]' - 2.yaml 3.yaml
jq: Bad JSON in --slurpfile .[0] == .[1] -: Could not open -: No such file or directory
```

This is linked to ArgumentParser's prefix matches as described in this [SO question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/33900846/disable-unique-prefix-matches-for-argparse-and-optparse).

Workaround: use `-s` instead of `--slurp`.

issue_title: Deprecate context managers?
issue_body:
I think introducing the pseudo-context-managers was a mistake.

They do not really provide a context if they don't reset the state afterwards.

Instead of this

```python
>>> with cleared(lcd):
>>>     lcd.write_string(u'LCD is cleared.')
```

...you could simply write...

```python
>>> lcd.clear()
>>> lcd.write_string(u'LCD is cleared.')
```

That's more explicit and makes more sense. Therefore I think the managers should be deprecated.

This would also kill #12.

issue_title: Add ca_certs config option
issue_body:
Flower has "certfile" and "keyfile" configuration options to specify a client certificate. However, it does not have a "ca_certs" option to specify a custom CA bundle to trust. This makes it difficult to connect to brokers with certs signed by a non-system authority.

The "ca_certs" key should be added to the ssl_options dict that is passed to Tornado.

issue_title: Url provider schemes selection
issue_body:
It could be very nice to have the possibility to generate url with control over the scheme chosen.

### Steps to reproduce
```python
from faker import Faker
fake = Faker()
print([fake.url() for i in range(10)])  # Statistically it will generate at least one 'https' url
# Output
['http://silva-choi.com/', 'http://lawson.net/', 'https://powell.com/', 'http://parker.com/', 'http://www.ortega.com/', 'https://www.davis.com/', 'http://maxwell.info/', 'http://lopez.com/', 'https://www.sanders.com/', 'http://www.baker-meadows.com/']
```
### Expected behavior
Something similar to this
```python
from faker import Faker
fake = Faker()
fake.url(http=False)  # Will generate only 'https' urls
fake.url(https=False)  # Will generate only 'http' urls
fake.url(https=True, http=True)  # Same as fake.url() (actual behaviour)
fake.url(https=False, http=False)  # Raise error
```
obviously this signature is purely for explaining the use case. Could be something like
```python
fake.url(exclude_scheme='http') # Will generate only 'https' urls
fake.url(exclude_scheme='https') # Will generate only 'http' urls
```
or with a enum
```python
fake.url(exclude_scheme=Scheme.HTTP) # Will generate only 'https' urls
fake.url(exclude_scheme=Scheme.HTTPS) # Will generate only 'http' urls
```

issue_title: pepper8 does not output HTML errors with the flake8-commas plugin
issue_body:
I have found a bug in the latest version of `pepper8`

1. Install flake8-commas via `pip install`.
2. Enable the errors by including C812 in a setup.cfg file, or with `--select`.
3. Run `flake8` to get text output.
4. Run `pepper8` on the output of `flake8`.

You will be able to see the standard `flake8` warnings and errors in the HTML output, but not the output resulting from plugins, which are present in the text output.

issue_title: Usage freezes terminal in Windows
issue_body:
Invoking `thefuck` in any way causes my terminal to lock up, using any shell--Powershell (via ConEmu) vanilla Powershell, or msysgit's git bash.

For example, `thefuck git brnch` outputs a slightly malformed suggestion of "git branch" and then stops accepting user input entirely.

I'm running Windows 10 and 32-bit Python 3.5.0.

Powershell

Git bash

Fail sox.file_info(): soxi binary does not exists in Windows
issue_body:
In Windows the last version of SoX (14.4.2) does not have `soxi.exe` binary, It looks like it uses the following command:

```
sox --i, --info              Behave as soxi(1)
```
The error:
```
>>> sox.file_info.duration('c:\\tmp\\test.flac')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Program Files\Python36\lib\site-packages\sox\core.py", line 125, in soxi
    shell=True, stderr=subprocess.PIPE
  File "C:\Program Files\Python36\lib\subprocess.py", line 336, in check_output
    **kwargs).stdout
  File "C:\Program Files\Python36\lib\subprocess.py", line 418, in run
    output=stdout, stderr=stderr)
subprocess.CalledProcessError: Command 'soxi -D c:\tmp\test.flac' returned non-zero exit status 1.
```


I have tried this patch and it works:
```diff
@@ -115,7 +115,7 @@
     if argument not in SOXI_ARGS:
         raise ValueError("Invalid argument '{}' to Soxi".format(argument))

+    args = ['sox --i']
-    args = ['soxi']
     args.append("-{}".format(argument))
     args.append(enquote_filepath(filepath))
```

Thanks!

issue_title: showing a AtomGroup loses local changes
issue_body:
When I show a AtomGroup the coordinates are reloaded from the trajectory. If I made any local changes for modeling or analysis they are lost and I can't check if any of my transformation had the desired effect. To reproduce this see code below.

```python
import MDAnalysis as mda
from MDAnalysisTests.datafiles import PSF, DCD
import nglview as ng

u = mda.Universe(PSF, DCD)
p = u.select_atoms('all')

# display 
pa = p.principle_axes()
p.translate(-p.center_of_geometry())
p.rotate(pa)

p.write('psf_in_pa.pdb')

# this will show the protein with the original loaded coordinates
view = ng.show_mdanalysis(p)
view

# now the protein is shown in the principal axes coordinates system
u2 = mda.Universe('psf_in_pa.pdb')
view2 = ng.show_mdanalysis(u2)
view2
```

I'm aware this is mostly a problem of MDAnalysis and the  current behavior is good to look at a trajectory. To look at a single AtomGroup this isn't ideal though. Adding a new keyword to `show_mdanalysis` to not reload the coordinates would be good solution.

issue_body:
Hi,
first off I'm a total noob on github so this is probably the wrong way to handle this. Secondly, big thanks for writing this library.

Now to the topic. 
I have a VeraSecure controller that is connected to Home Assistant that is using your library. The VeraSecure have an built in siren that is triggered with the different modes. It can also be turned on and off via Luup calls. BUT the siren is now added as a generic device that can be armed. 

The siren is not armed in that sense in Vera but tightly connected to the modes. 

To automate siren and the usage of the siren the siren should be treated as a switch.

In __init__.py 
When initiating all devices the siren is not recognised by the right category and is initilized as a VeraDevice. 

My suggestion is to add  the correct category  on row 32

CATEGORY_POWER_METER = 21
CATEGORY_VERA_SIREN = 24
CATEGORY_UV_SENSOR = 28

And add the correct device class for it.  Rows 160-170isch 

                elif device_category == CATEGORY_SCENE_CONTROLLER:
                    device = VeraSceneController(item, self)
                elif device_category == CATEGORY_VERA_SIREN:
                    device = VeraSwitch(item, self)
                else:
                    device = VeraDevice(item, self)
                self.devices.append(device)
                if device_category != CATEGORY_SWITCH and device_category != CATEGORY_VERA_SIREN and device.is_armable:
                    self.devices.append(VeraArmableDevice(item, self))
            else:
                self.devices.append(VeraDevice(item, self))



Im runnig this myself and I can now controll and detect when the siren is invoked with no other changes.

Let me know if this is interesting and if you need more information.

Thanks for great job on this,
Patrik Ekström

issue_title: Very small triangles pass through merge_vertices but cause issues in normal computation
issue_body:
When using `triangles.normals()` the cross product of two sides of the triangle is first calculated, then unitized in `util.unitize()`.

When calculating this cross product, it is possible for vertices that are too far apart to be merged in preprocessing under the standard 1e-8 `TOL_MERGE` to result in sides with a cross product with norm  smaller than the 1e-13 `TOL_ZERO` specified in util.py

For example, a triangle with all sides having length 1.1e-8 will not be merged, but the cross product of two sides has length 1.1e-8 ^ 2 * sin (pi/3) = 1.0e-16, which is zero according to TOL_ZERO.

This causes `triangles.normals()` to deem the face invalid even though it passes through `base.merge_vertices()`.

This is mostly a problem in `apply_transform`, where `normals_pre` and `normals_post` can be invalid if the first face falls under this condition, which breaks `triangles.windings_aligned()` and causes an exception.

issue_title: Auto-retry failing tasks with rescheduling
issue_body:
As a follow-up to #50, I want to share the code snippet I ended up using to implement infinite auto-retry for the crashing tasks with rescheduling the tasks back through RabbitMQ queue, which may help if the task will just get scheduled to a non-failing worker. I could not find a way to implement non-infinite auto-retry with rescheduling using RabbitMQ since you cannot update the existing task with some meta information (e.g. a number of failing attempts), but using some external service (e.g. Redis or Memcached), you can modify this code to implement any retry logic you want, so I leave it as an exercise to the reader to adapt the code snippet.

```python
import functools
import logging

from kuyruk import Kuyruk as BaseKuyruk, Task


log = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def safe_kuyruk_task(func, retry_delay):
    """
    Args:
        func (callable): the wrapped function task.
        retry_delay (float): time in seconds between retries on crash.
    """
    @functools.wraps(func)
    def decorator(*args, **kwargs):
        try:
            return func(*args, **kwargs)
        except (Kuyruk.Discard, Kuyruk.Reject):
            raise
        except Exception:
            log.exception("Task has crashed... Retrying in %g seconds.", retry_delay)
            raise Kuyruk.Reject()

    return decorator


class Kuyruk(BaseKuyruk):

    def task(self, queue='kuyruk', retry_delay=60, **kwargs):
        """
        Wrap functions with this decorator to convert them to *tasks*.
        After wrapping, calling the function will send a message to
        a queue instead of running the function.

        Args:
            queue: Queue name for the tasks.
            retry_delay (float): time in seconds between retries on crash.
            kwargs: Keyword arguments will be passed to :class:`~kuyruk.Task`
                constructor.

        Returns:
            function: Callable :class:`~kuyruk.Task` object wrapping the
            original function.
        """
        def inner(func):
            return Task(
                    safe_kuyruk_task(func, retry_delay),
                    self,
                    queue,
                    reject_delay=retry_delay,
                    **kwargs)

        return inner


kuyruk = Kuyruk()
```

I had to override `inner` implementation of the Kuyruk class to pass my `safe_kuyruk_task` adapter, which enables the auto-retry logic for all the tasks automatically (thus, no changes to the existing tasks codebase is needed except this change of Kuyruk initialization).

@cenkalti I couldn't find a good way to generalize this thing, so I won't send a PR, but I decided to share my code since other may wonder how to do that. It would be great to see some upstream solution to this problem either in form of some built-in implementation or just as an example/documentation somewhere.

issue_title: Shouldn't string functions be None-safe?
issue_body:
I found that string functions aren't consistently `None`-safe, i.e. `trunc`, `camel_case`, ... are `None`-safe but `explode` and others aren't.

I propose string functions to be `None`-safe as is in [Apache Commons Lang](http://commons.apache.org/proper/commons-lang/javadocs/api-release/index.html) and in [underscore.string](https://github.com/epeli/underscore.string).

I also propose: `_.to_string(None)` returns `''` instead of `'None'` actually.

issue_title: Unable to clone GitLab repos
issue_body:
I seem to be hitting a race in `GitlabService`'s initialization:
```
$ git repo -v lab clone guyzmo/git-repo
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "…/git_repo/repo.py", line 347, in do_clone
    service = RepositoryService.get_service(repository, self.target)
  File "…/git_repo/services/service.py", line 126, in get_service
    cls._current = service(repository, config)
  File "…/git_repo/services/ext/gitlab.py", line 22, in __init__
    super().__init__(*args, **kwarg)
  File "…/git_repo/services/service.py", line 168, in __init__
    self.connect()
  File "…/git_repo/services/ext/gitlab.py", line 26, in connect
    self.gl.set_url(self.url_ro)
AttributeError: 'GitlabService' object has no attribute 'gl'
```

A brief look at the code suggests the problem lies in the execution order:
- `GitlabService.__init__` calls `RepositoryService.__init__`
  - `RepositoryService.__init__` initializes `RepositoryService.insecure`
  - `RepositoryService.__init__` calls `GitlabService.connect`
    - `GitlabService.connect` accesses `GitlabService.gl`
- `GitlabService.__init__` initializes `GitlabService.gl` using `RepositoryService.insecure`

The other services swap the two lines in the constructor, but here we can't do that.

I'm using git-repo 1.8.0 on Python 3.6, installed via `pip install --user git-repo`. `list` and `create` both work fine.

issue_title: Backpoff mode for TCP client
issue_body:
Right now when StatsD is down `TCPStatsClient` will try to establish TCP connection while handling each metric which is definitely unnecessary when it goes to use of resources and network saturation.

Idea is to either implement simple logic like wait 1 second between each re-try or make it pluggable so would be easy to implement f.ex. exponential backoff.

issue_title: Add support for openvr_driver.h and the driver_lighthouse library
issue_body:
The [openvr_driver.h](https://github.com/ValveSoftware/openvr/blob/master/headers/openvr_driver.h) and driver_lighthouse library are normally used by vrserver, but can be used directly.

This permits, for example, using tracked devices even when you can't run the usual vrserver, or prefer not to.  For robots, and non-SteamVR stacks, and such.

The API is similar but different, with some additional interfaces like  [IServerTrackedDeviceProvider](https://github.com/ValveSoftware/openvr/wiki/IServerTrackedDeviceProvider_Overview).

And happily, it is already running on linux.  For example, Vrui's [OpenVRHost.h](https://github.com/KeckCAVES/Vrui/blob/master/VRDeviceDaemon/VRDevices/OpenVRHost.h) and [OpenVRHost.cpp](https://github.com/KeckCAVES/Vrui/blob/master/VRDeviceDaemon/VRDevices/OpenVRHost.cpp) using this [openvr_driver.h](https://github.com/KeckCAVES/Vrui/blob/master/Contributed/OpenVR/headers/openvr_driver.h).  And for node.js, there's [steamvr-lighthouse-driver](https://github.com/mncharity/node-webvr-alt-stack/tree/master/packages/steamvr-lighthouse-driver/src/addon).

It would be nice to use openvr devices from python, without having to run Steam's vrserver.

But should that be done with pyopenvr?  Parts of openvr.h, and their associated python glue, are needed for both.  But it is a separate underlying library.  In node.js, these would be several little modules.  For python, I'm not sure: should it be a new pyopenvr-driver, with some code sharing or duplication; or a bigger pyopenvr?

issue_title: Segmentation evaluation - boundary detection: to trim or not to trim / special segment labels
issue_body:
Quite frequently, ground-truth annotations contain leading and/or trailing silence, typically (e.g. in SALAMI) annotated using special segment labels like 'Silence'. When such a special segment label appears at the front, the start time of this segment does not contain a real boundary which boundary detection algorithms would find, conversely for ending times of silent end segments. 
Currently, it seems that the segment.detection function must be tricked by introducing very short silent segments, if those are not already present in the ground-truth, to consistently trim them, or not.

issue_title: WSGI strings should be decoded with ISO-8859-1 on Python 3
issue_body:
### Problem

When running my Django app with bjoern and navigating to an URL with characters outside of ISO-8859-1 charset it fails with error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/webapps/django-interfax-app/env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django/core/handlers/wsgi.py", line 145, in __call__
    request = self.request_class(environ)
  File "/webapps/django-interfax-app/env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django/core/handlers/wsgi.py", line 69, in __init__
    path_info = get_path_info(environ)
  File "/webapps/django-interfax-app/env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django/core/handlers/wsgi.py", line 162, in get_path_info
    path_info = get_bytes_from_wsgi(environ, 'PATH_INFO', '/')
  File "/webapps/django-interfax-app/env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django/core/handlers/wsgi.py", line 210, in get_bytes_from_wsgi
    return value.encode('iso-8859-1')
UnicodeEncodeError: 'latin-1' codec can't encode character '\u0457' in position 1: ordinal not in range(256)
```

### Probably the root cause

> On Python platforms where the str or StringType type is in fact Unicode-based (e.g. Jython, IronPython, Python 3, etc.), all "strings" referred to in this specification must contain only code points representable in ISO-8859-1 encoding (\u0000 through \u00FF, inclusive). [1]

[1] https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3333/#unicode-issues

### Expected

As it's implemented in [`wsgiref`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/wsgiref/simple_server.py#L85).

```python3
def application(environ, start_response):
    start_response('200 OK', [])
    print('PATH_INFO:', environ['PATH_INFO'])
    yield b'OK'

from wsgiref.simple_server import make_server
httpd = make_server('0.0.0.0', 8080, application)
httpd.serve_forever()
```

`curl localhost:8080/%C3%A5` prints `PATH_INFO: /Ã¥`.

### Actual

```python3
def application(environ, start_response):
    start_response('200 OK', [])
    print('PATH_INFO:', environ['PATH_INFO'])
    yield b'OK'

import bjoern
bjoern.run(application, '0.0.0.0', 8080)
```

`curl localhost:8080/%C3%A5` prints `PATH_INFO: /å`.

issue_title: Embedded IPython shell hangs on ctrl-D if colorama.init() has been called
issue_body:
Windows 10.0.15063 Pro
Python 2.7.14
IPython 5.6.0
colorama 0.3.9

## Normal behavior

1. Execute this code:
```python
import IPython

IPython.embed()
```
2. Press ctrl-D
: Do you really want to exit ([y]/n)?
3. Press Enter
: shell exits

## Reproduce

1. Execute this code:
```python
import IPython
import colorama

colorama.init()
IPython.embed()
```
2. Press ctrl-D
: Terminal hangs
: Windows popup: "python2.exe has stopped working"

## Analysis
This init function messes with sys.stdout like this:
```python
def init(autoreset=False, convert=None, strip=None, wrap=True):
...
    sys.stdout = wrapped_stdout = \
        wrap_stream(orig_stdout, convert, strip, autoreset, wrap)
```
Commenting out that assignment fixes the problem.
This is what wrap_stream looks like:
```python
from .ansitowin32 import AnsiToWin32

def wrap_stream(stream, convert, strip, autoreset, wrap):
    if wrap:
        wrapper = AnsiToWin32(stream,
            convert=convert, strip=strip, autoreset=autoreset)
        if wrapper.should_wrap():
            stream = wrapper.stream
    return stream
```

issue_title: Design issue for repeating the events across multiple modules
issue_body:
I'm starting to have a lot of functions implemented in my bot, and I feel it's just poor design to have methods such as `on_message` which do 20 different things in the same function block.

I wish to split responsibilities among different modules which would all have the capabilities of the Client class but while sharing the same instance.

I made an example to illustrate my point:

```
# File bot.py
class Bot(discord.Client):   
    def __init__(self):
        self.modules = [Module1()] # Initialize all modules
        super().__init__()

    # This method is broadcast, it will also be called on every module attached using the same 
    # parameters.
    @event_broadcast    
    async def on_message(self, message: discord.Message):
        # Here I have access to the Client fields, e.g. client.servers:
        for server in self.servers:
            print("I am connected to server %s!" % server.name)
        # However, most modules have different actions upon receiving a message, so I let each
        # module implement its own logic
        
# File module1.py
class Module1():
    async def on_message(self, message: discord.Message):
        # Here I don't have access to the client fields because Module doesn't inherit from 
        # Client... I can pass a reference to the Client instance, but that creates circular 
        # dependencies.
        pass

```

I'm not sure whether I am missing a design pattern that would solve my problem or if I am limited by the design of library to revolve around a single instance.

issue_title: Exception on program exit using SSL in python >= 3.4 ?
issue_body:
I think this will hit anyone connecting to an SSL broker on python > 3.4, but not sure.   If someone else can reproduce it, that would be good.

I connect to a broker, and without SSL, the code works fine.  When SSL is on, it still works fine, but as part of sys.exit() processing it prints out this:

Exception ignored in: <bound method _AbstractTransport.\_\_del\_\_ of <amqplib.client_0_8.transport.SSLTransport object at 0x7f93e0834dd8>>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/peter/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/amqplib-1.0.2-py3.5.egg/amqplib/client_0_8/transport.py", line 97, in \_\_del\_\_
  File "/home/peter/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/amqplib-1.0.2-py3.5.egg/amqplib/client_0_8/transport.py", line 136, in close
  File "/home/peter/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/amqplib-1.0.2-py3.5.egg/amqplib/client_0_8/transport.py", line 208, in _shutdown_transport
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/ssl.py", line 971, in unwrap
    s = self._sslobj.unwrap()
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/ssl.py", line 642, in unwrap
    return self._sslobj.shutdown()
ssl.SSLError: Underlying socket connection gone (_ssl.c:2020)
b

Nothing in the application seems responsible for this.  It just always happens in sys.exit()

I found two methods of suppressing the error:
   in amqplib/client_0_8/transport.py

I read a bit, and it seems that in python >= 3.4 it has gotten more consistent about calling \_\_del\_\_()

issue_title: KafkaConsumer.poll()  never receives first message on a topic
issue_body:
When the consumer is initialized without a group id it can never receive the first message written to a topic.

```
from __future__ import absolute_import, division, print_function
from kafka import KafkaConsumer, KafkaProducer
import time

timeout = 100
topic = 'test.10'

consumer = KafkaConsumer(topic, group_id=None, bootstrap_servers='localhost:9093')
producer = KafkaProducer(bootstrap_servers='localhost:9093')

def poll():
    partitions = consumer.poll(timeout)
    if len(partitions) > 0:
        for p in partitions:
            for response in partitions[p]:
                print(response)
    else:
        print("poll() - no results found")            

def produce():
    result = producer.send(topic, 'hello')
    md = result.get()
    print("send() - %s" % str(md))
    
poll()  # set high water mark
produce()
poll()  # get the value
poll()  # try again, just for the heck of it
produce()
poll()  # second produce result is found

---------------
bash$ python /tmp/consumer.py 
poll() - no results found
send() - RecordMetadata(topic=test.10, partition=0, offset=0)
poll() - no results found
poll() - no results found
send() - RecordMetadata(topic=test.10, partition=0, offset=1)
ConsumerRecord(topic=u'test.10', partition=0, offset=1, key=None, value='hello')

bash$ python /tmp/consumer.py 
poll() - no results found
send() - RecordMetadata(topic=test.10, partition=0, offset=2)
ConsumerRecord(topic=u'test.10', partition=0, offset=2, key=None, value='hello')
poll() - no results found
send() - RecordMetadata(topic=test.10, partition=0, offset=3)
ConsumerRecord(topic=u'test.10', partition=0, offset=3, key=None, value='hello')
```

First run, when before the topic was created the consumer does not receive the first message, but receives messages thereafter.

Second run the first poll() sets the high water mark and subsequent records are found.

Setting the topic to a new topic causes the above results to be repeatable.

issue_title: await queue.get() seems to gets stuck when no timeout is set
issue_body:
What should be the correct behaviour in the following case :

```
async def consumer(queue_in):
    while True:
        msg = await queue_in.get()
        await do_stuff(msg)
```

From what I observe is that the queue just gets stuck waiting for an element. But when an element is added to the queue, the `get()` doesn't "see" it, and keeps waiting: `2017-09-07 15:13:50,959 aio_pika.queue  DEBUG    Awaiting message from queue: <Queue(queue_in): auto_delete=False, durable=None, exclusive=False, arguments=None>`

However after adding the timeout:

```
async def consumer(queue_in):
    while True:
        try:
            msg = await queue_in.get(timeout=3.0)
            await do_stuff(msg)
        except TimeoutError:
            await asyncio.sleep(1.5)
```

The `get()` times out (even when an element is added to the queue within the timeout), and in the next iteration it reads all the elements from the queue added in the meantime.

Am I doing something wrong ?

issue_title: Higher-level abstractions for setting SPI transfer_size
issue_body:
This is a feature request, not a bug report.

Currently, the command line parsers and device configuration settings for the MAX7219 devices in the luma library allow the SPI bus speed (i.e. clock speed) and CS active-high to be set, but there's no way to adjust the maximum `transfer_size` parameter, which is exposed by the `spi` class constructor (the default is 4096). I think this would be very useful for me to debug my glitchy MAX7219-based displays; the raspberry pi supports 4096, but I think the cascaded MAX7219s in my display would be happier with smaller words. I don't want to have to manually override the default parameter in the core library, it would be great if this could be supported from the command line argument parser.

issue_title: term.number_of_colors raises if term._does_styling is False
issue_body:
I've identified this as the root cause of:
https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1425836

I have a formatter that calls term.number_of_colors. This normally works, but if `term._does_styling` is False, then we never call `setupterm`. This means the call to `tigetnum` in the `number_of_colors` property will fail. We should probably just return 0 in this case.

issue_title: Generating indicators for different frequencies
issue_body:
Just came across your repo and it looks very promising for what I'm doing! Really impressive that you generated all of these indicators. 

It would be great to be able to generate indicators for different frequencies - month, week, hour, minute. Have you looked into this at all? This would be useful for day trading. Then users could do:

```
dataframe.get('rsi_1_H')
dataframe.get('rsi_30_min')
dataframe.get('rsi_15_min')
```

It could be a backwards compatible change in that if no frequency is specified, daily frequency is used. Otherwise specify the frequency using the [Pandas offset alias format](https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/timeseries.html#offset-aliases). 

The data frame would have to have the highest frequency asked for (minute-level for minute frequency, etc.). The library would have to be able to determine the frequency of the data,
 and [resample](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/version/0.17.0/generated/pandas.Series.resample.html) the data to generate indicators for the higher time series. 

I can look into this more also if you can offer thoughts on how feasible you think this is and what pieces of code I should look at.

issue_title: API UWI numbers losing their leading zeros etc - being parsed as ints
Recent release creates very hard to debug errors
issue_body:
This commit https://github.com/lixxu/sanic-jinja2/commit/cd1cb9747cce1ed66a25d8582fd73a25c52a3929 is incomplete. It is incomplete because when an app is calling `init_app` later in their code, it will not make aware of the default `app_name` argument being set to `None`.

Therefore it will pass `None` to the Jinja2 Loader which then throws a cryptic `ImportError`:

```
File "/media/ephemeral0/ben/app.aca9088a3a8cc36fc7bddd72ee32e56c12edc24c/app/__init__.py", line 83, in make_app
  jinja2_env.init_app(app)
File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/sanic_jinja2/__init__.py", line 31, in init_app
  loader = PackageLoader(pkg_name, 'templates')
File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jinja2/loaders.py", line 224, in __init__
  provider = get_provider(package_name)
File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 435, in get_provider
  __import__(moduleOrReq)
TypeError: __import__() argument 1 must be str, not None
```

How is anyone going to debug this? 

Suggested remediation:

- Add an exception for this case (`if app_name is None: raise ValueError('need an app name!')`)

and/or

- `if app_name is None: app_name = app.name`

issue_title: Inserting numbers with bind parameters fails with Oracle 11.2 with HY000
issue_body:
# Summary
Attempting to use bind parameters for number values fails.  e.g.  

cursor.execute("INSERT INTO foobar (ID) VALUES (?)", [5])

gets the following response:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
pyodbc.Error: ('HY000', 'The driver did not supply an error!')

**I have reproduced this on two completely separate systems.  **

Note: this appears to have similarities to #94 , but I can reproduce it with just a single column.  

# Detailed Example
## Setup
Table foobar looks like:
`SQL> describe foobar;`

Name                |  Null?   | Type
------------------- |  -------- |-----------------------------
ID                     |             | NUMBER(10)
WORDS           |             | VARCHAR2(2048)
STUFF             |              | CLOB
CNT                 |             | NUMBER(3)
TXT                  |             | NVARCHAR2(32)

Create a connection in Python:
`import pyodbc`
`px = pyodbc.connect("""my connection string""")`
`pc = px.cursor()`

## Activity and Responses
Start with an insert statement without bind parameters.  
> pc.execute("insert into foobar values (1,'first row', 'little clob', 3, 'foo')")
>   <pyodbc.Cursor object at 0x0000000002606450>

so that worked.  no surprise there.  

Now try a single bind parameter with a number.
> pc.execute("insert into foobar values (?,'2d row', 'ltl clob', 33, 'food')",[2])
> Traceback (most recent call last):
>   File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
> pyodbc.Error: ('HY000', 'The driver did not supply an error!')

**THAT ILLUSTRATES THE ERROR.  Binding to a number failed.**

How about binding to a string...
> pc.execute("insert into foobar values (3,'2d row', 'ltl clob', 33, ?)",['woof'])
>  <pyodbc.Cursor object at 0x0000000002606450>

That worked fine.  So it is not all binds...

Same behavior with multiple binds to numbers ? 
> pc.execute("insert into foobar values (?,'2d row', 'ltl clob', ?, 'food')",[2,33])
> Traceback (most recent call last):
>   File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
> pyodbc.Error: ('HY000', 'The driver did not supply an error!')

**Yep, same issue with multiple binds**

How about mixing binds to numbers and strings ?
> pc.execute("insert into foobar values (?,'2d row', 'ltl clob', 42, ?)",[11,'baz'])
> Traceback (most recent call last):
>   File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
> pyodbc.Error: ('HY000', 'The driver did not supply an error!')

**That fails too.   If there is a number being bound, it fails....**


# More platform specifics:
Oracle 11.2.0.1.0 
pyodbc 3.0.10
python 3.4.0 (v3.4.0:04f714765c13, Mar 16 2014, 19:25:23) [MSC v.1600 64 bit (AMD64)]
Windows 7 Professional, SR1

# Open Questions
* Is this only with Oracle?   Only 11.2....?
* Is this specific to Windows ?   
I do not have the resources to test against other combinations of platforms

When I execute a select-Statement that contains a string parameter (``NCHAR`` in Oracle) I do not get any results if the length of the parameter and the length of the database field do not exactly match. 

See this minimal example:

```
""" create table foo
    (
        colnum    number(20)    not null,
        colchar1  nchar(6)      not null,
        colchar2  nchar(20)     not null
    );

    insert into foo values (1, 'foobar', 'foobar');
"""

import pyodbc

conn = pyodbc.connect("...")
curs = conn.cursor()


curs.execute("select * from foo;")
print(curs.fetchall()) # Works

curs.execute("select * from foo where colnum = ? and colchar1 = ?;", [ 1, "foobar"] )
print(curs.fetchall()) # Works

curs.execute("select * from foo where colnum = ? and colchar2 = ?;", [ 1, "foobar"] )
print(curs.fetchall()) # Does not work
```

This does only happen for Oracle Databases. I have tested this with Postgres 9.6.2 and the issue does not come up. I have yet to test this on a Linux machine and maybe try Oracle 12c - but I don't expect changes.

issue_title: Upload proper distutils/setuptools tarball to PyPI
issue_body:
Dear numdifftools developers,
I have recently packaged numdifftools 0.9.16 for Gentoo. One problem is that you do not provide full distutils tarballs on PyPI anymore. Linux distributions heavily rely on being able to run distutils/setuptools on their own systems, be it Gentoo or any other distribution, and hence wheels packages are unworkable for us. So please also upload properly packaged tarballs to PyPI (including all the metadata files such as PKG-INFO) and so. And no, the Github generated tarballs are not sufficient, as they lack the PKG-INFO files, which results in an error. The wider Linux community will greatly appreciate having both tarballs and wheels packages! Thanks for considering my call for help.

issue_title: Improve browser reload behavior
issue_body:
As noted by @adamcik and others, a browser reload may mess up the library (and also playlist) view, since the currently displayed directory will become the new "root", with no "back" button being shown.
Although this shouldn't be a problem on a mobile devices (and therefore is unlikely to be addressed in ionic), it's still a nuisance when testing.
A simple workaround might be to automatically "redirect" to the library (or playlists) root when no `backView` is available in the current tab's history stack. This could be checked in an `$ionicView.loaded` event handler, for example (would be nice to do this in a state `resolve`, but the view and its back view are not available there).
Care must be taken regarding view caching, but since all "resolve" views are not cached for now, this shouldn't be much of an issue.

issue_title: General color cannot be overridden in module config
The future and i3bar vesion 4.12
issue_body: With the release of i3bar 4.12 things are going to go a bit crazy ``` response = { 'full_text': 'Blue text is cool!', 'background': "#FF00FF', 'markup': 'pango' } ``` If I have i3bar 4.12 I have new exciting formatting, if not then I get a horrible bit of raw html. I think it would be nice to keep compatibility with old and new versions of both i3bar and py3status the simplest way I can see to do this is to make responses like ``` response = { 'full_text': 'this is legacy output', 'pango': 'New output', } ``` py3status could then use pango if we have i3bar >= 4.12 else use full_text. We could also strip any tags from full_text to stop people just using that for pango output. seeing as i3bar ignores stuff it doesn't know sending the 'html' bit of response to it for legacy py3ststus would be fine. Anyhow this is my thoughts on how to deal with this change. What do others think?

import platform

issue_title: `cwd` messes up the working dir of the caller after exiting `with`
issue_body:
Example Python script:

```python
import tempfile
import os
import plumbum

print("Intial cwd:", os.getcwd())
os.chdir(tempfile.mkdtemp())
print("changed dir to:", os.getcwd())
print("Calling plumbum.cwd()...")
with plumbum.local.cwd('/dev/'):
    pass
print("BUG! The cwd is now back to what it was at the start:", os.getcwd())
```

The problem seems to be here: https://github.com/tomerfiliba/plumbum/blob/53ae73708e9bbb2e520415dd066343321a000b6a/plumbum/path/local.py#L308

`self._path` is cached when the instance is constructed, which happens to be at import time. I think instead `__call__` should call `os.getcwd()` at that line, to get the most up-to-date path.

As a side note, calling `os.chdir()` means that the cwd change will be visible to other threads running in the same process, which makes plumbum not thread safe. If instead plumbum could pass the new path through to the `cwd` named parameter of `Popen`, that might be safer. I haven't read enough of the code to know whether that's practical though.

issue_title: grids module : status and next features
issue_body:
The grids module is now in a state where I intend to leave it, until Rich is back. This issue is to describe how I set it up and what needs to be done next. It is very possible it'll need a refactor - but its in a state where we can immediately figure this out when you're back!

In Scripts, check out the 'grid_demo.py' file to see how grids work with code ;).

**Grids**

The grids module essentially contains a set of structured NumPy arrays, which hold the (x,y) coordinates of an image. Coordinates *are held as either : 

_GridRegular_ - a NumPy array where the coordinates correspond to a uniform regular grid. This has shape ndarray[image_pixels, 2] 

_GridSub_ - a NumPy array where the coordinates correspond to the sub-grid of each pixel. This has shape ndarray[image_pixels, sub_pixels, 2] 

Grids which correspond to regions of the data inherit from the above two classes:

_GridImage_ - The regular grid of unmasked image pixels.

_GridImageSub_ - The sub grid of unmasked image pixels.

_GridBlurring_ - The regular grid of pixels which are outside the mask, but need to be included in the analysis due to PSF blurring effects.

Finally, there is a _GridCollection_, which stores all of the above grids and is the object passed to the ray-tracing module for lensing calculations.

I anticipate we will have other grids added in the future, for different types of pixelizations / calculations.

**GridData and GridMapper**

In addition to the grids above, which take care of coordinates, we'll also have:

- _GridData_ - This grid follows the same structure as _GridRegular_ e.g. it is of shape [image_pixels]. It does not contain coordinates, but data instead (e.g the image fluxes, noise-map, etc.). We'll ultimately be comparing the images we make on _GridRegular_ to the actual data, thus its ideal that they share the same structure.

_GridMapper_ - This maps pixel indexes between grids. Currently, the code has a _GridMapperSparse_, which maps each image pixel in _GridImage_ to their nearest pixels defined on a sparse uniform grid (e.g. if grid_mapper.image_to_sparse[4] = 3, then the 5th image pixel maps to the 4th sparse pixel). This is used purely for optimization, which I suspect most other _GridMappers_ will be.

Currently, we can't map any of our grids, whether they be _GridData_, _GridRegular_ or _GridSub_ back to their original 2d images. This is straight forward to add, and requires a _GridMapper_ to be added to do this. I'll have probably added this by the time you read this!

**Computing Deflections and Intensities**

_GridRegular_  has functions which:

1) Take in a list of galaxies.
2) Compute the deflections / intensities for all mass / light profiles in each galaxy.
3) Return the deflection angles / intensities in the same structure as the grid (noting the grid's last dimension is 2 as it is an (x,y) grid, so for a grid of computed intensities this dimension is dropped).

_GridSub_ has functions which do the same, but:

- If it is computing deflection angles, the complete set of deflection angles on every sub-pixel are returned.
- If it is computing intensities, each set of sub-pixel intensities are averaged to compute the overall intensity in the pixel.

These choices reflect the fact that a sub-grid of deflection angles is required for setting up the next grid of sub-pixels and performing the source reconstruction, whereas there is no merit in keeping track of sub-pixel intensities as only the pixel intensities can be compared to the actual image data.

Deflection angles can be computed simultaneously across all grids at the _GridCollection_ level.

**Ray Tracing and Grids**

The ray_tracing module receives an instance of _GridCollection_ as its input, as well as a list of galaxies. It groups the galaxies into different planes in the ray-tracing calculation:

- _ImagePlane_ - The first plane in the ray-tracing calculation, corresponding to the lowest redshift galaxy(s). Note that because this is the image-plane, its coordinates correspond to the **regular uniform grid** of the CCD imaging data.

- _LensPlane_ - A plane in the ray-tracing calculation where the grid coordinates have been lensed by a foreground galaxy(s) and are thus not on a uniform grid. This is not the last plane, so the deflection angles at the plane's coordinates are computed such that they can be traced to the next plane.

- _SourcePlane_ - The last plane in the ray-tracing calculation. Because it is the last plane, no deflection angles are computed as there is not another plane to trace the coordinates to.

To set up each plane, the deflection angles are computed in each plane using the _GridCollection_ deflection angle functions. Each **Plane** therefore has two _GridCollection_s - a collection of (traced) image coordinate and collection of deflection angles at those coordinates.

To keep things simple, the ray_tracing module currently assumes just two planes, an image-plane and source-plane. In the future, we'll want to implement multi-plane lensing, where the distribution of galaxy redshifts dictates the number of planes, and the angular diameter distances separating these planes determines the deflection angles.

I have made sure the way I've designed this code won't hinder us setting up multi-plane lensing. My suspicion is it will make it so that we store the planes as a list, as opposed to explicitly calling them image_plane and source_plane as we do now.

**Generating Images from a Plane**

Once ray-tracing is compute, and we therefore have an instance of the _RayTrace_ class, it is now possible to generate what the images of each plane of galaxies looks like. A given plane is comprised of: (i) grids of coordinates and; (ii) galaxies with light profiles. Generating its image requires us to evaluate all of the plane's galaxy light profiles at its grids of coordinates, which is performed by passing the galaxies into our **Grid**s.

Computing the deflection angles on each grid (e.g. image, sub, blurring) is straightforward. For each grid, whether it be a _GridImage_, _GridImageSub_, or _GridBlurring_, all you do is compute the deflection angles due to each plane's galaxies at the coordinates defined by the grid.

For generating a galaxy image, via its light profile and intensities, things are not so straight-forward. This is because there are multiple ways that one might want to compute the intensities from our _GridCollection_:

- Using _GridRegular_, thus ignoring sub-pixel gridding.

- Using _GridSub_, such that each intensity is the average of the sub-pixel intensities over that pixel.

In light of this, I have added to the light_profiles module a class, _LightProfileSettings_. This allows one to customize, for each light profile instance, how its intensities are computed from an input gird. Thus, we could have a galaxy where the first light profile is computed using _GridRegular_ and second using _GridSub_ (this isn't an unrealistic occurrence).

Here is where it gets tricky, as when we go to each plane in the ray-tracing instance, we need to use its galaxies to go to their light profiles and correctly choose each grid which it uses. The code currently uses intensity calculations in the **Galaxy** class, thus this may require us to filter light profiles.

Whilst both methods of computing intensities above are implemented in the code, the code does not choose its method based on the _LightProfileSettings_. I figured that was the point where refactoring a bad solution could take time, so stopped there. Currently, the code defaults to always use _GridRegular_.

**Iterative Sub-grid**

There is a third approach to computing intensities (which is also controlled by _LightProfileSettings_). Here, _GridRegular_ is used in-conjunction with and an iterative sub-grid, whereby intensity values are computed on iteratively higher resolution sub-grids until the change in value reaches a threshold value of precision. In the centre of a light profile, sub-grids of order (1000 x 1000) may be necessary, which are clearly too high resolution to be set up using _GridSub_.

In the geometry_profiles module, you've already coded up something to this effect, albeit not using the _GridRegular_ data structure.

This approach is only valid when the coordinates are on a regular uniform grid, which is true only when they are in the _ImagePlane_. Thus, it should be turned off for any other grid, regardless of _LightProfileSettings_.

There are also circumstances where we may wish to evaluate a light profile in the image-plane, even though it traces to a higher redshift _LensPlane_ or _SourcePlane_. In this case, we will still assign the galaxy to the higher redshift **Plane**, but will evaluate its light profile using the _ImagePlane_ grid. In _LightProfileSettings_, I have added a variable 'image_plane_override' to control this behaviour. It is not yeet implemented in ray_tracing, but I'd guess the _ImagePlane_ class could have an extra input in addition to galaxies called something like 'extra_light_profiles'.

**MappingMatrix**

Above, we're using light profiles to generate images. If a _Plane_ has a _Pixelization_, we will instead use the plane coordinates to generate a _MappingMatrix_. This is the matrix we've discussed and used in the past, which maps every image pixel to source pixel using 0s and 1s (or fractional values for a sub grid). This isn't implemented yet, as the pixelization module has some work to do, but it should be fairly straightforward to implement as it doesn't have the confusing issues light profiles do above.

**Analysis**

So, once this is all set up and working, the point is we can use the ray_tracing module to:

- Generate model images of all galaxies, which we can analyse by comparing to the image data (e.g. blurring with the PSF, subtracting from the image data to compute residuals, evaluating a goodness of fit).

- Generate the mapping matrices of our pixelizations, which we can blur with the PSF and fit the image data with.

This will take place in some form of 'analysis' module, which I'll start writing on Friday :).

issue_title: README.md comments on satisfy_general seems wrong
issue_body:
The comments around satisfy_general say:
```python
# General caveats are verified by arbitrary functions
# that return false if the caveat is not met
# and true if it is met or not understood
```

The statement `and true if it is met or not understood` does not seem to match the implementation. The implementation is such that it should return False if not understood. As implemented, If any satisfy_general callback returns True the macaroon is valid.

Example code:
```python
from pymacaroons import Macaroon, Verifier

def foo(predicate):
    print("foo", predicate)
    return True

def bar(predicate):
    print("bar", predicate)
    return False

m = Macaroon(key='key', location='location', identifier='identifier')

m.add_first_party_caveat('key1 = value1')
m.add_first_party_caveat('key2 = value2')

v = Verifier()
v.satisfy_general(foo)
v.satisfy_general(bar)

try:
    print(v.verify(m, 'key'))
except Exception as e:
    print(e)
```

Even though bar returns False, the macaroon is "valid" since foo returns True, which is expected according to the implementation but goes against the comment.

The implementation currently matches libmacaroons python bindings as well.

So I think the comment just needs to be updated to say to return True if the predicate is both understood and met, otherwise false.

issue_title: --exclude/--exclude-pattern can't be used at the same time as --include
issue_body:
When passing --include, all --exclude/--exclude-pattern parameters are ignored and file matching only considers if a file is in any of the include paths or not.

The following seems like a pretty valid usecase that would be nice if it was supported:

    coveralls --include src --exclude src/not-this-file.c --exclude-pattern '.*useless.*'

General questions on how to do something with the XlsxWriter module should be
asked on [StackOverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/xlsxwriter).
Add the ``xlsxwriter`` tag to the question. This has a better chance of

in the future.


See below for information on adding a Bug Report or Feature Request.


## Reporting Bugs

workbook.close()

```

Notes:

1. Ensure that the example code can be run to generate a file that

2. Only include code that relates to the issue.
3. Remove non-relevant text from this template.
4. If you are seeing an issue in LibreOffice, OpenOffice or another third
   party application, also test the output with a version of Excel.
5. You can get the required version numbers as follows:

        python -c 'import xlsxwriter; print(xlsxwriter.__version__)'

issue_title: Repositories without slashes in path are ignored.
issue_body:
Parsing any URL which doesn't contain a `/` in the path fails.

For example, this is correct (even if the 'owner' part isn't generally meaningful):

```
>>> giturlparse.parse('ssh://user@example.com/path/repo.git')
Parsed(pathname='/path/repo.git', protocols=['ssh'], protocol='ssh', href='ssh://user@example.com/path/repo.git', resource='example.com', user='user', port=None, name='repo', owner='path')
```

But this yields nonsensical results:

```
>>> giturlparse.parse('ssh://user@example.com/repo.git')
Parsed(pathname='/user@example.com/repo.git', protocols=['ssh'], protocol='ssh', href='ssh://user@example.com/repo.git', resource='ssh', user=None, port=None, name='repo', owner='user@example.com')
```

This, again, is correct:

```
>>> giturlparse.parse('user@example.com:path/repo.git')
Parsed(pathname='path/repo.git', protocols=[], protocol='ssh', href='user@example.com:path/repo.git', resource='example.com', user='user', port=None, name='repo', owner='path')
```

issue_title: Password keys 1 and 2 are the same
issue_body:
self.pw and self.pw_scalar are the "same". They need to be cryptographically different and not dependent on the other:
```
self.pw_scalar = params.group.password_to_scalar(hkdf(pw, "pw1"))
self.pw = hkdf(pw, "pw2")
```
This is especially true for the client-server augmentation of SPAKE2, PAKE2+.
```
self.pw_scalar = params.group.password_to_scalar(hkdf(pw, "pw1"))
self.pw = hkdf(pw, "pw2")
self.pw3 = params.group.password_to_scalar(hkdf(pw, "pw3"))
```

issue_title: Make ObjectProxy Picklable
Feature request: allow interval jobs to be offset by a random value
issue_body:
When jobs are configured to execute at certain times, they tend to coalesce at one particular time. For example, executing 10 jobs at the top of the hour, another 10 once in at noon and once at midnight, and a third job that executes every minute will cause all of these jobs to run at the same time at noon and midnight, potentially causing a bottleneck in the number of threads.

This could be solved by introducing a new parameter to the interval trigger that would define a value in seconds by which each job would be offset.

Assuming these new imports:
``` python
from operator import add, sub
from random import randrange, choice
```
The constructor could have a new parameter value (`random_offset`) that would be at most equivalent to the configured interval:
``` python
self.random_offset = min(self.interval_length, random_offset)
```
Then, the `get_next_fire_time()` function could check if an offset is defined and modify the next execution time accordingly:
``` python
if self.random_offset:
    offset = timedelta(seconds=randrange(0, self.random_offset))
    op = choice((add, sub))
    next_fire_time = max(datetime.now(self.timezone), op(next_fire_time, offset))
```
I understand all this can be worked around by setting custom start times for each group of jobs so that they don't intersect but having an easy-to-configure parameter would be cool too. The UNIX Anacron scheduler uses a similar mechanic with the [`RANDOM_DELAY` option](http://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man5/anacrontab.5.html).

I did notice during testing of the above code that `get_next_fire_time()` is called both from `_get_run_times()` (in `job.py`) and `_process_jobs` in (in `base.py`), producing different times for each. I'm not sure if it's significant that these functions both receive the same next fire time.

Anyway, is this something you'd consider merging if I were to submit a PR? Thank you!

issue_title: The `_merge_dict` method overrides nested dict
Bug in filetype detection for archive entries
issue_body:
The bug is in libarchive/adapters/archive_entry.py, in the ArchiveEntry class' filetype property, specifically line 99:
> `flags = dict([(k, (v & filetype) > 0)`
This should change to:
`flags = dict([(k, (v & filetype) == v)`

The problem is that some of the IF* flags have overlapping bits set, so multiple flags will return `(v & filetype) > 0` as being True. For example, `IFREG = 010000`, `IFLNK = 0120000`, and `IFSOCK = 0140000`; so if the file is ANY one of these types, all 3 flags will be set.

issue_title: wait() always expects Pattern object as argument
issue_body:
Hi Jon,
Thanks for this project, can't wait to get rid of the Java dependencies.
I'm trying to convert my sikulix scripts and found this tiny issue.

Lackey's `wait()` always expects a Pattern object as an argument so you cannot do `wait(2)` as you would with Sikulix (see e.g. [this](https://answers.launchpad.net/sikuli/+question/235023)):

```
>>> wait(2)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "lackey\RegionMatching.py", line 462, in wait
    raise FindFailed("Could not find pattern '{}'".format(path))
lackey.Exceptions.FindFailed: Could not find pattern '2'
```
I saw this was actually implemented in `exists()`, `waitVanish()` and `findAll()`, but the type-checking misses floats:
```
>>> exists(2)
>>> exists(2.3)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "lackey\RegionMatching.py", line 522, in exists
    raise TypeError("find expected a string [image path] or Pattern object")
TypeError: find expected a string [image path] or Pattern object
```

issue_title: needs to be made thread safe
issue_body:
I notice there are a number of places in watchdog that arn't properly thread safe. For example:

  EventDispatcher._dispatch_events()
        event, watch = event_queue.get(block=True, timeout=timeout)
        self.dispatch_event(event, watch)

This code path is not thread safe, as the watch object could get removed by the time the "dispatch_event" method obtains it's lock.

When running into this, it results in a KeyError and the resulting thread dying, thus watchdog no longer monitors a path. I had a traceback of this, but I've misplaced it at the moment, will past it it the next time I see it.

issue_title: Linearize curves and surfaces to a tolerance
issue_body:
It looks like curvepts is made by proceeding at a sample rate of _delta from 0 to 1 proportionally along the whole length of the spline.  

For use in CAM we want the geometry subdivided only as far as necessary that the the approximation fits the the curve within the margin of a tolerance value.  Often we will set this to the tolerance of the CNC machine, but we don't want to overdo it, because the controllers tend to choke on too many close together points.

issue_title: wattime behavior
issue_body:
Thanks for all of the effort you've put into this library. It's the only one that I've found to be actively developed and work well.

There is a bug with respect to either the logic or exception message for waittime in the PingTransmitter class:

```
       if self.waittime <= 0:
            raise ValueError(
                "wait time must be greater than or equal to zero")
```

When `waittime` is set to `0`, the exception is raised, which states that the 

> wait time must be greater than or equal to zero

According to the exception, `0` should be a valid value, but it isn't

More to the point, what prompted me to start digging into this is that I was looking for a way to exclude the use of the `-w` flag on Linux altogether. I want ping to return as soon as it's done.

As a feature request in addition to the bug above, it would be very helpful to be able to set waittime to `0` and have the `-w` flag not be passed to ping.

issue_title: Delete Service Object from a rule
Add RelatedField
issue_body:
Sometimes it's necessary to link on model to another such as:

```python
class Product(SimpleModel):
    fields = ('name', 'brand',  'attributes')
    attributes = fields.RelatedField(ProductAttribute)


class ProductAttribute(SimpleModel):
    fields = ('name', 'description', 'value')
```

It makes easier to create composed models as above by just doing:

```python
>>> product_data = {
...    'name': 'foo prod',
...    'brand': 'bar brand',
...    'attributes': [{
...        'name': 'baz attr',
...        'description': "i'm a baz attribute",
...        'value': 69
...    },
...    {
...        'name': 'qux attr',
...        'description': "i'm a qux attribute",
...        'value': 6.9
...    }]
...}

>>> product = Product(**product_data)
>>> product.attributes
[ProductAttribute(name='baz attr', ...), ProductAttribute(name='qux attr', ...)]
```

issue_title: Remove `_func` in all single-objective function names
issue_body:
Currently, our single-objective functions look like this:

```python
def sphere_func(x):
    """Documentation"""
    j = x ** 2
    return j
```

We realized that it's much better to remove the `_func` part in the name.

```python
# Expected output
def sphere(x): # Just remove "_func!"
    """Documentation"""
    j = x ** 2
    return j 
```

## Where to apply the changes?
1. All single-objective functions reside in the `pyswarms/utils/functions/single_obj.py. Simply change the function names.
2. However, some function calls are present inside the `tests/` module. Hunt them down!
3. You also need to double-check our documentation in `pyswarms/docs/*` and `README.md`. Make sure that the API is up-to-date with our docs and examples!

If you have any questions, do not hesitate to ping us!

issue_title: ORDER BY FIELD(name, val, val, val...)
issue_body:
As suggested by @danielroseman.

As of Django 1.8, `order_by` can take expressions - https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/ref/models/querysets/#order-by , thus the `FIELD` function ( https://mariadb.com/kb/en/mariadb/field/ ) should be implemented, which can be used for getting results ordered in a specific fashion. This tutorial covers it: http://www.electrictoolbox.com/mysql-order-specific-field-values/ . 

It may also be useful to supply a shortcut that puts the unspecified values at the end, rather than the beginning, as noted in the second half of the tutorial. It could maybe be done without the double ordering forwards and backwards by using an IF, i.e. something like `IF(@tmp:=FIELD(x, val1, val2) > 0, @tmp, 99999999999)`. Might be faster that way.

sock.setsockopt(socket.IPPROTO_TCP, socket.TCP_NODELAY, 1)

give RTT 0.004 (i.e. 234 msg send/recv ops/sec)

issue_title: Using stSort with a function not working
issue_body:
We are using smarttable 1.4.5 with AngularJS 1.2.26 now.
After upgrading we are seeing an issue when using a function for sorting is ignored and sorting is not working at all. Using a plain predicate is working as expected.

```
<table st-table="displayedCollection" st-safe-src="phonenumbers" class="table table-hover" table-title="Phonenumbers">
<th st-sort="getters.sortNumber" st-skip-natural>{{'phoneNumber.title' | translate}}</th>
```

After debugging the issue I found the check in the stSort directive link function whether the predicate is a function or not does not see the function in it's scope, hence always returning false and leaving the predicate as a string. I'm not sure why ng.isFunction fails initially, which may also be an issue with angular itself.

As a possible solution we patched the smarttable controller to do the check when sortBy is called.
I will provide a pull request for review.

issue_title: Chaining/inheritance/sourcing?
issue_body:
I think it would be handy to provide a way for a `.env` file to include a "base" file with a bunch of default values, that it can then override. A couple thoughts on how it might work:

* Use "source", like in bash. Just put this line at the top of `.env`

`
source "./defaults.env"
`

And all the values in `defaults.env` get set as if they were entered in `.env` itself, with later entries overriding the earlier ones. You would probably also want an `unset` keyword to remove inherited values.

That's probably the simplest approach. If you wanted to get fancy, you could make the inheritance explicit. Something like:

```
base "./defaults.env"

MY_VALUE="xyz"          # Sets this variable, as normal
inherit OTHER_VALUE   # Sets this variable to the value specified in defaults.env. Doesn't set any other values.
```


In other words, you would have to explicitly list which variables to inherit. You could also have `inherit all` and `unset` keywords.

I would probably lean towards the first approach, just for simplicity's sake.
Thoughts? I'd be happy to contribute a patch.

A better way would be to use a config file or a `.netrc` file for credentials.

issue_title: Segmentation fault when using threading module
issue_body:
The following code produces a segmentation fault:

```python
import threading
from pyroaring import BitMap

def compute():
    bm = BitMap()

t = threading.Thread(target=compute)
t.start()
t.join()
```

It seems that the crash happens when the destructor of `BitMap` is called.
If we call any method of the `BitMap` object after its initialization (e.g. a call to `len(bm)`), then the crash is produced by this call.

Nothing bad happens if the `BitMap` is created before the creation of the thread.
For instance, the following code exits without error:
```python
import threading
from pyroaring import BitMap

bm = BitMap()

def compute():
    bm.add(42)

t = threading.Thread(target=compute)
t.start()
t.join()
print(bm)
```

issue_body:

I'm trying to (re)bind an abstract class (who inherits from abc.ABC) to a mock instance created with MagicMock with the aim of test a class who inherits from MethodView, I'm doing to rebind calling FlaskInjector into the setUp() method:

```
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from unittest import TestCase
from unittest.mock import MagicMock

from flask import Flask
from flask.json import jsonify
from flask.views import MethodView
from flask_injector import FlaskInjector
from injector import inject

class ServiceInterface(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def do_action(self) -> str:
        pass

class Endpoints(MethodView):
    @inject
    def __init__(self, service: ServiceInterface):
        self.service = service

    def get(self):
        return jsonify(self.service.do_action())

class Test(TestCase):
    def conf(self, binder):
        self.srv_mock = MagicMock(autospec=ServiceInterface)
        binder.bind(ServiceInterface, self.srv_mock)

    def setUp(self):

    def test_mock(self):

        r = self.client.get('/endpoint/')
        self.assertEqual(r.status_code, 200)

def configure_bindings(binder):
    pass

app = Flask('app')
app.add_url_rule('/endpoint/', view_func=Endpoints.as_view('ep'), methods=['GET',])
FlaskInjector(app=app, modules=[configure_bindings])
```

And fail with an error:

```

injector.CallError: Call to ABCMeta.__new__() failed: Can't instantiate abstract class ServiceInterface with abstract methods do_action (injection stack: [<class 'flask-injector-mock.Endpoints'>])

issue_title: python wrapper speed
issue_body:
Hi,

Thanks for a great library! I considered switching to parasail-python  (from SSW) in my projects due to the global alignment implementation. However, I observed a ~10-20x slowdown compared to ssw (using [this wrapper](https://github.com/vishnubob/ssw) of the ssw implementation). I was hoping that this speed difference happens in the conversion from python to C, and could easily be fixed. Below is the code I use. 

Any help regarding this is much appreciated!

```
import parasail
from time import time
import ssw  # using python wrapper from https://github.com/vishnubob/ssw

s1 = ""
s2 = ""

start = time()
user_matrix = parasail.matrix_create("ACGT", 2, -1)
result = parasail.nw_trace_diag_16(s1, s2, 2, 0, user_matrix)
total_elapsed = time() - start
print("parasail", total_elapsed)

start = time()
user_matrix = parasail.matrix_create("ACGT", 2, -1)
result = parasail.ssw(s1, s2, 2, 0, user_matrix)
total_elapsed = time() - start
print("parasail-ssw", total_elapsed)

start = time()
score_matrix = ssw.DNA_ScoreMatrix(match=2, mismatch=-1)
aligner = ssw.Aligner(gap_open=2, gap_extend=0, matrix=score_matrix)
result = aligner.align(s1, s2, revcomp=False)
total_elapsed = time() - start
print("ssw", total_elapsed)
```

gives 
```
('parasail', 0.04381084442138672)
('parasail-ssw', 0.09930706024169922)
('ssw', 0.004579067230224609)
```

issue_title: Animated trisurf with variable number of vertices and ipywidgets
issue_body:
Hello hello,

first off, I like the library, nice work so far! However, I encountered something I want to do, but can't.

I have a timeseries of 3D density data, and I want to make animated contours/isosurfaces/density plots (same concept, different jargon). I make the surfaces with [scikit-image's marching cubes](http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/api/skimage.measure.html?highlight=isosurface#skimage.measure.marching_cubes_lewiner). And of course I want to plot them with ipyvolume. Plotting a single using `plot_trisurf` works just fine. The trouble comes with animating the whole thing.

The surfaces have a different number of vertices and triangles, so I can't use the build-in animation capabilities. Instead, I elected to do the animation controls with ipywidgets (also because they're shiny).
The first thing I tried was to create all Meshes first, setting their `visible` to `False`, and making the animation loop toggle the correct one. This works for small cases, but uses way to much memory on the JS side to work for my case. 
The next attempt was to create the appropriate Mesh on change, but I haven't found a way to remove the previous Mesh (and after drawing 10 on top of each other my memory craps out).

So, my questions: is there a convenient way to 1) animate trisurf's with different numbers of vertices (#90); or 2) remove specific Meshes from the figure; or 3) clear all meshes from the figure without modifying the scene limits or the camera?

Keep up the good work, ipyvolume is one of the three 3D plotting libraries I found with a quick search (next to Mayavi (pain to install/doesn't work on Py3) and Plot.ly)! And I like yours better so far :)

issue_title: Issue in CoxPHFitter._newton_rhaphson
issue_body:
I am trying to use the CoxPH Fitter and I have been having a lot of troubles running the regression.
When running the code

    cf = CoxPHFitter()
    cf.fit(hidden_clinical_data, duration_col = 'duration', event_col = 'observed',
             include_likelihood=True, show_progress = True
            )

hidden_clinical_data is a Panda Dataframe of shape 260 x 502 (of all non-null numbers)
When I run the `cf.fit` line, I get the error:

    //anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/lifelines/fitters/coxph_fitter.pyc in _newton_rhaphson(self, X, T,    E, initial_beta, step_size, precision, show_progress, include_likelihood)
    240             i += 1
    241 
    --> 242         self._hessian_ = hessian
    243         self._score_ = gradient
    244         if include_likelihood:
    UnboundLocalError: local variable 'hessian' referenced before assignment

I ran through the code with the python debugger to try and get to the bottom of it. What I discerned is that the reason why this error is raised is because of the control structure at line 229.
Before the point of failure, the norm of the delta is still > 10 (line 227)
It then executes line 228 and the step_size was finally reduced to be < 0.001
It then continues and exits the while loop.

In this control, the program never reaches line 233 to actually set the variable `hessian`
And so when it breaks from the while loop, it reaches an error when executing line 242. 

Is this the intended behavior of the program? Is there something inherently wrong with the data for the assumptions of norm(delta) > 10 while step size < 0.001?

What could solve this solution?

issue_title: ObjectDoesNotExist exception
issue_body:
Hi,

I have a nested router set up, and the `retrieve`-method raises a `ObjectDoesNotExist` exception instead of `NotFound`, so DRF doesn't pick it up:

My view:
```
from rest_framework import viewsets
from rest_framework.response import Response
from arch.utils.jsend import JSendMixin

from ..models import Actor
from .serializers import ActorSerializer

class ActorViewSet(JSendMixin, viewsets.ModelViewSet):
    queryset = Actor.objects.all()
    serializer_class = ActorSerializer


class ActorByOrgViewSet(ActorViewSet):
    def list(self, request, org_pk=None):
        queryset = self.queryset.filter(org=org_pk)
        serializer = ActorSerializer(queryset, many=True)
        return Response(serializer.data)

    def retrieve(self, request, pk=None, org_pk=None):
        try:
            queryset = self.queryset.get(pk=pk, org=org_pk)
        except ObjectDoesNotExist:
            raise NotFound()

        serializer = ActorSerializer(queryset)
        return Response(serializer.data)
``` 
(-Note the `except ObjectDoesNotExist` workaround, I would love to get rid of it!)

and `urls.py`:
```
from django.conf.urls import patterns, include, url
from rest_framework.urlpatterns import format_suffix_patterns
from rest_framework_nested import routers

from org.rest.urls import orgs_router
from .views import ActorViewSet, ActorByOrgViewSet

actors_router = routers.DefaultRouter()
actors_router.register(r'actors', ActorViewSet)

actors_by_org_router = routers.NestedSimpleRouter(orgs_router, r'orgs', lookup='org')
actors_by_org_router.register(r'actors', ActorByOrgViewSet)

urlpatterns = [
    url(r'^', include(actors_router.urls)),
    url(r'^', include(actors_by_org_router.urls)),
]
```
Where the `orgs_router` imported looks like this:
```
from django.conf.urls import patterns, include, url
from rest_framework.urlpatterns import format_suffix_patterns
from rest_framework_nested import routers

from .views import OrgViewSet

orgs_router = routers.DefaultRouter()
orgs_router.register(r'orgs', OrgViewSet)

urlpatterns = [
    url(r'^', include(orgs_router.urls)),
]
```

I'm pretty new to all this, so might be s.th I've missed.

issue_title: Submit Worksheet to competition
issue_body: **Issue by [lr292358](https://github.com/lr292358)** _Wednesday Apr 29, 2015 at 15:48 GMT_ _Originally opened as https://github.com/codalab/codalab-competitions/issues/994_ ---- We need means of submitting worksheets to competitions. There are two ways, the first is easier and the second is better. 1) [P1] Semi-manual way: the result .zip produced by the worksheet that is to be submitted to the competition side encodes the worksheet (e.g. in a separate certificate file); the competition program can check and accept only valid URI. Certificate file = e.g. hash(hidden_permutation(URI) + hash(zip)), so people cannot use other worksheet with particular results generated. 2) [P2] Fully automatic way (best): Create a feature: submit the worksheet results to a competition from command line. Link worksheets on competition results and public submission boards.

issue_title: Descriptive payload validation error messages
issue_body:
Hi there, great work on Flask-Restful so far, keep the updates coming :) I wrote before about the possibility of using custom JSON schema objects for validation in #88 In 0.8.1 you added all the validation properties to fields of RestPlus models, so I can switch to using the native models for payload validation. This is great, however in the process I noticed one problem. When I was using my hacked custom schema validation with Flask-RestPlus 0.8.0, then it produced HTTP 400 errors with a very descriptive JSON message such as: ```json { "errors": { "int_field": "'test' is not of type 'integer'" }, "message": "Input payload validation failed" } ``` or ```json { "errors": { "int_field": "1 is less than the minimum of 100" }, "message": "Input payload validation failed" } ``` or ```json { "errors": { "string_field": "'test' is too short" }, "message": "Input payload validation failed" } ``` Curently with 0.8.1 the same code produces the much less descriptive HTML: ```html 400 Bad Request

Bad Request

The browser (or proxy) sent a request that this server could not understand.

``` I'm sure it would be possible to extract and return the validation errors in the form of JSON. This can be then used by the API clients to display error messages to users. It would be great if you could take a look at this.

issue_title: Add Facility Management?
issue_body:
I wanted to open a discussion on facility management in this app.  Essentially I see some limitations in how this app can be useful for more than 1 type of websocket unless all the application logic sits on the client side (javascript) code. More to the point:

* It's not documented how to restrict allowed facilities.
* It's not terribly easy to allow or restrict channels based on the facility being called.
* There is no way on the server side to hook into a received message, or other important events such as connected and closed (maybe even publish).

The final bullet point being my biggest motivation here. I read a bit where other users of this app hinted at this limitation and were quickly met with a response such as you shouldn't need to do that; hinting because concurrent Python code is tricky, and most server-side logic will be blocking calls and thats bad for websockets running on gevent. I agree concurrent Python code is tricky, and I admit I do not fully understand it myself. But I also think this app shouldn't hard-prevent us from add anything in (even though we may shoot ourselves in the foot).

I'd like to propose the following; and I'm totally willing to contribute the code, but I also somewhat fear this is a big change to the core values you had when writing this app and the contribution may be faced with opposition (hence this discussion).

* Make a Facility class, having it be more than the simple string it is currently.
* This would look much like the ws4redis class on the javascript side, but on the server, containing the functions on_open, on_close, on_message. The BaseFacility class would define these functions, but they will be empty.
* Add hooks from the RedisSubscriber/RedisStore into the defined facility class where applicable.
* Define allowed_channels per facility rather than a global list. Right now it seems quite messy to allow more than 1 type of facility (especially if we want basic server side logic).
* In settings we could define a list WS4REDIS_FACILITIES much like context processes or installed apps.

A quick code example to show what I was thinking..
```python
WS4REDIS_FACILITIES = (
    'chat': 'mysite.apps.chat.facility.ChatFacility',
    ...
)
```

```python
class ChatFacility(BaseFacility):

    def __init__(self, websocket, redis, user):
        pass

    def on_open(self):
        pass

    def on_message(self, message):
        pass

    def on_close(self):
        pass

    @classmethod
    def allowed_channels(request, channels):
        return channels
```
