issue_title: AdWordsReportError is missing information from _ExtractRequestSummaryFields
issue_body:
Hi,

I have a program that receives a report definition from the user and runs it on Adwords.
I've encountered a situation where the report failed and the exception is missing a good explanation for the failure, although this data exists in the headers of the response.

Example of the report: `{"selector":{"fields":["AccountCurrencyCode"]},"reportType":"GEO_PERFORMANCE_REPORT","includeZeroImpressions":"false"}`

I'm running the `report_downloader.DownloadReportAsString` and getting the following error:
 `AdWordsReportError('AdWords report download failed with HTTP status code: 400',)`.

In the log I see the following line: 
`googleads.adwords.report_downloader: Request Summary: {'clientCustomerId': 'xxx', 'includeZeroImpressions': 'false', 'server': 'adwords.google.com', 'skipColumnHeader': 'True', 'skipReportHeader': 'True', 'skipReportSummary': 'True', 'isError': True, 'errorMessage': '<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="yes"?><reportDownloadError><ApiError><type>SelectorError.MISSING_FIELDS</type><trigger>Must choose at least 1 field from the group [CountryCriteriaId]</trigger><fieldPath></fieldPath></ApiError></reportDownloadError>'}`

So I know we have more data about the error.
After digging in the code I found that the error is on the function: `_DownloadReportAsStream` on the line: `response = self.url_opener.open(request)`.
The logger writes the exception from the function `_ExtractRequestSummaryFields` but the exception is using the function `_ExtractError`.

Is there a way to add this kind of information to the `AdWordsReportError`?

issue_title: Add info about layer sha / rev to .build.manifest
issue_body: `charm-build` currently creates a `.build.manifest` file with info about what was built.  It would be good to include in that the specific rev or sha for each layer that was built, so that it can be audited exactly what went into building the charm.

issue_title: Make it possible to run filter-fasta.py on a file of AA sequences
issue_body:
If you try to run `filter-fasta.py` on a file of AA sequences, you get an error:

```
ilter-fasta.py --titleRegex 'herpesvirus.*glycoprotein g ' < viral.1.protein.faa
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/terry/.virtualenvs/dm3/bin/filter-fasta.py", line 12, in <module>
    exec(compile(open(__file__).read(), __file__, 'exec'))
  File "/Users/terry/dark-matter/dark-matter/bin/filter-fasta.py", line 78, in <module>
    head=args.head, removeDuplicates=args.removeDuplicates):
  File "/Users/terry/dark-matter/dark-matter/dark/reads.py", line 542, in filter
    for readIndex, read in enumerate(self):
  File "/Users/terry/dark-matter/dark-matter/dark/reads.py", line 453, in __iter__
    for read in chain(self.iter(), self.additionalReads):
  File "/Users/terry/dark-matter/dark-matter/dark/fasta.py", line 118, in iter
    read.checkAlphabet(count=None)
  File "/Users/terry/dark-matter/dark-matter/dark/reads.py", line 183, in checkAlphabet
    str(self.__class__.__name__)))
ValueError: Read alphabet ('ADEFGHIKLMNPQRSTVY') is not a subset of expected alphabet ('ACGT') for read class DNARead.
```

It would be nice if the user didn't have to specify this on the command line - though that might be the simplest thing to implement. We could use a `try/except` with different read types to see what worked, but it might be better to force the user to be explicit?

issue_title: Add a filter option called maxAlignmentsPerRead to ReadsAlignments
issue_body: This will allow us to filter out reads that don't match anything (or in the more general case reads that match only up to a certain number of subjects).

issue_title: Retrieving Card Registration on stateless backend
issue_body:
When creating a card registration you have to first create an instance on the server using the SDK, send the information down to the browser to use the card registration JS library and then back to the server to complete the registration. This is fine if you can keep the card registration object in memory, but if you're using a stateless backend it doesn't appear to be a way to retrieve the card registration object back with the SDK. 

Could I propose a solution that allows a user to store the id of the card registration in a database and then recall its ID and use it to recall a previous card registration like so?

```
recalled_card_registration = CardRegistration(id=recalled_id)
```

issue_title: images.build failing on container build output
issue_body:
The ImageCollection.build() method parses the `docker build` output to extract the name of the image being built by looking for the string "Successfully built {sha256}".  Some commands in a Dockerfile can also produce this string causing the build() method to return early with an incorrect image name.

Example output from a `RUN pip install cffi` command:
```
 ...
Building wheels for collected packages: cffi, pycparser
  Running setup.py bdist_wheel for cffi
  Stored in directory: /root/.cache/pip/wheels/ea/df/5c/7d11cd4eec7b94be3b0ec4ed0076e0f38846a22c7200576149
  Running setup.py bdist_wheel for pycparser
  Stored in directory: /root/.cache/pip/wheels/bc/07/7e/cd81c0b23417ebaed9eb2584c00bd539871d5309a7a2324953
Successfully built cffi pycparser
...
```

A partial fix is to make the regex used to search for the string more restrictive by adding start and end anchors.  This can still result in some false positives.
A more complete fix only checks the last line of output for the "Success..." string.
PR in progress.

issue_title: support `--since` on docker logs
issue_body:
The docker CLI has a `--since` option on `docker logs` where you can provide a timestamp for the logs.
This is really useful in scripting, 

    print all logs
    last_timestamp = timestamp of last log
    ... do stuff ...
    print all logs since timestamp

There is really no way to achieve this right now without grabbing all the logs, and manually throwing out all the old ones.

issue_title: Request feat(controller): add i/o limits
issue_body:
Currently is possible to do something like this:

```
docker run -it --rm --name block-device-test gliderlabs/alpine:3.1 /bin/sh

wget http://www.iozone.org/src/current/iozone3_430.tar

apk-install build-base

tar xpvf iozone3_430.tar
cd iozone3_430/src/current
make linux-AMD64

./iozone -l 32 -O -i 0 -i 1 -i 2 -e -+n -r 4K -s 1G
```

Per se this is not "wrong" but it will consume all the disk i/o available and start a chain of events that it could put the node in a unusable/inaccessible state for the duration of the task.
Currently in docker/libcontainer there is some work to add support to limit the i/o but is not yet available as a docker flag:

https://github.com/docker/libcontainer/pull/354
https://github.com/docker/libcontainer/pull/337

issue_title: ETCD Requires Persistent Storage
issue_body:
When running a 5 node cluster, stopping and starting a node causes the cluster to fail.

Using the cloud formation scripts do the following:

* Boot a 5 node cluster in amazon.
* Ensure etcd is running and has consensus.
* Stop a node (thus wiping out ephemeral storage).
* Start the Node.

Expected: Cluster Unaffected, Node Rejoin
Actual: Cluster-wide failure, Ephemeral disk fails to mount, All nodes crash etcd.

Solution?

Don't use ephemeral storage for etcd.

issue_title: bug(database): the backup should not interfere the etcd publication
issue_body:
If the backup time is bigger than the etcd timeout the database becomes unavailable.
A side effect of this is that the service status is ok (active - running) without any flapping.

This is the output from a node with this problem:
```
  PID TTY      STAT   TIME COMMAND
24393 ?        S      0:00 -bash
24501 ?        R+     0:00  \_ ps axf
    1 ?        Ss     0:00 /bin/bash /app/bin/boot
  110 ?        S      0:00 sudo -i -u postgres /usr/lib/postgresql/9.3/bin/postgres -c config-file=/etc/postgresql/9.3/main/postgresql.conf -c listen-addresses=*
  117 ?        S      0:00  \_ /usr/lib/postgresql/9.3/bin/postgres -c config-file=/etc/postgresql/9.3/main/postgresql.conf -c listen-addresses=*
  120 ?        Ss     0:01      \_ postgres: checkpointer process
  121 ?        Ss     0:00      \_ postgres: writer process
  122 ?        Ss     0:00      \_ postgres: wal writer process
  123 ?        Ss     0:00      \_ postgres: autovacuum launcher process
  124 ?        Ss     0:00      \_ postgres: archiver process   last was 000000020000000400000044
  125 ?        Ss     0:00      \_ postgres: stats collector process
  111 ?        Sl     1:03 confd -node 192.168.2.201:4001 -config-file /app/confd.toml
 4867 ?        S      0:00 sudo -u postgres envdir /etc/wal-e.d/env wal-e delete --confirm retain 5
 4868 ?        Sl     0:05  \_ /usr/bin/python /usr/local/bin/wal-e delete --confirm retain 5
```

How is possible to reproduce this? 
Any timeout during the upload of the backup ( `wal-e`) produces this output:
https://gist.github.com/aledbf/43e2c19344f812f7411f
wal-e retry the upload and it waits forever

This behavior avoids the periodic publication of the host/port in etcd
`https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/database/bin/boot#L147`

#2701 solves this behavior

issue_title: Add automatic retries to request methods
issue_body:
We have been considering adding support for retry logic on all HTTP request methods, built into the SDK.
However, there are a few caveats and gotchas.

Replies May Change
---

  * Not all requests are safe/idempotent to retry.

    Some POST, PUT, and DELETE methods, like task submission, are carefully built to allow retries in the event of network failures.

    Not all APIs are so carefully put together -- and in some cases the semantics of a retry may be safe, but differ from a successful request. For example, an API may accept `POST /some/path`, and 200 on the first request, but 403 on subsequent `POST`s. Follow-up requests may even have strange semantics like "restart processing of this request".

  * Even safely retry-able requests may have different responses on retry.

    Even if something is fine and safe, a second submission may give a 204 instead of a 200. It may even do nothing new, but return a 403 or 404, which we would translate into an error. This seems especially true of `DELETE`s, which should generally be safe to retry, but very reasonably can 404 after the first request arrives -- I would call that behavior convergent, rather than idempotent. Semantically, everything is fine, but we will "stupidly" translate the 404 into an error, and likely surprise someone.

  * Related things may be changing service-side.

    I'm not sure if this opens up new issues or not, but server-side state may change the semantics of a request between retries. Consider task label updates, which are not allowed on completed tasks, but are allowed on incomplete tasks. The initial update may network error (but arrive and succeed), followed by a retry which fails because the Task completes.
    Even though clients already should be accounting for the error raised, they will likely (incorrectly) assume that the request failed in its entirety.

The Caller Still Needs to be Ready for Failure
---

It's always possible for the retries to be exceeded, and then we're back in the same situation, demanding that the caller has good error handling.

Less Flexible than Client Retries
---

Whatever the client to the SDK is, it will be limited by whatever we suggest.
For example, someone might have a network that makes exponential backoff for network failures highly desirable to reduce congestion, and someone else might want linear backoff for their switch which drops 1/2 of all packets. No matter what choice we make, it will likely be the wrong choice for someone down the line.

Easier to Add than Remove
---
Once we've added retries, it's hard to wean people off of our retry logic.
They will start relying on it for the stability of their own applications.
If we do this, it means we need to do it right.

issue_title: Request for auto commit option on the connection
issue_body:
It would be very handy to have an option on the `connect` function to tell the database to commit after every SQL statement (i.e. set SQL_ATTR_AUTOCOMMIT).  This would save having to call `commit()` all the time.  The default would be not to, to comply with [PEP249](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/#commit), but it would be good to be able to set this attribute explicitly.  Better still it would be good to be able to change this attribute dynamically, e.g. switch on "auto-commit" on the connection after the connection is established and then switch it off again later without dropping the connection.  I appreciate this attribute and behaviour may not be supported by all RDBMS's, but this is possible and does work with MS SQL Server.

For an example of an existing implementation, see [pyodbc](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki/Connection#autocommit).

### What is wrong?

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/venv/bin/trinity", line 11, in <module>
    load_entry_point('py-evm', 'console_scripts', 'trinity')()
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 542, in load_entry_point
    return get_distribution(dist).load_entry_point(group, name)
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2569, in load_entry_point
    return ep.load()
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2229, in load
    return self.resolve()
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 2235, in resolve
    module = __import__(self.module_name, fromlist=['__name__'], level=0)
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/trinity/__init__.py", line 10, in <module>
    from .main import (  # noqa: F401
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/trinity/main.py", line 29, in <module>
    from trinity.chains import (
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/trinity/chains/__init__.py", line 30, in <module>
    from trinity.config import (
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/trinity/config.py", line 26, in <module>
    from trinity.nodes.base import (
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/trinity/nodes/base.py", line 13, in <module>
    from trinity.rpc.main import (
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/trinity/rpc/__init__.py", line 1, in <module>
    from .main import RPCServer  # noqa: F401
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/trinity/rpc/main.py", line 9, in <module>
    from trinity.rpc.modules import (
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/trinity/rpc/modules/__init__.py", line 4, in <module>
    from .evm import EVM  # noqa: F401
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/trinity/rpc/modules/evm.py", line 5, in <module>
    from evm.tools.fixture_tests import (
  File "/home/ubuntu/py-evm/evm/tools/fixture_tests.py", line 10, in <module>
    import pytest
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pytest'
```


### How can it be fixed

Move `pytest` imports to within the function contexts that use them so that pytest isn't a hard dependency.  Wrap these imports in a `try/except` and raise a friendly error message when `pytest` is not available for import.

> The <function-name> tool requires pytest.  Please install the pytest library

issue_title: Race condition with `wait_for_transaction_receipt`
issue_body:
* Populus Version: 2.2.0
* OS: osx

### What was wrong?

When deploying a contract to Rinkeby, and immediately starting a wait for the transaction receipt, an exception is raised:

```
gateway/contract/tests/e2e/e2e_test_deploy.py:9 (test_deploy)
gateway/contract/tests/e2e/e2e_test_deploy.py:36: in test_deploy
    contract_address = chain.wait.for_contract_address(deploy_tx_hash)
../../.virtualenvs/ethereum-xPw_EAxE/lib/python3.5/site-packages/populus/wait.py:33: in for_contract_address
    txn_receipt = self.for_receipt(txn_hash, **kwargs)
../../.virtualenvs/ethereum-xPw_EAxE/lib/python3.5/site-packages/populus/wait.py:47: in for_receipt
    return wait_for_transaction_receipt(self.web3, txn_hash, **kwargs)
../../.virtualenvs/ethereum-xPw_EAxE/lib/python3.5/site-packages/populus/utils/wait.py:94: in wait_for_transaction_receipt
    poll_interval_fn=lambda: poll_interval if poll_interval is not None else random.random(),
../../.virtualenvs/ethereum-xPw_EAxE/lib/python3.5/site-packages/populus/utils/wait.py:77: in poll_until
    value = poll_fn()
../../.virtualenvs/ethereum-xPw_EAxE/lib/python3.5/site-packages/web3/eth.py:192: in getTransactionReceipt
    [transaction_hash],
../../.virtualenvs/ethereum-xPw_EAxE/lib/python3.5/site-packages/web3/manager.py:96: in request_blocking
    raise ValueError(response["error"])
E   ValueError: {'code': -32000, 'message': 'unknown transaction'}
```

The code in question is:

```
Token = chain.provider.get_contract_factory('Token')
deploy_tx_hash = Token.deploy(
    transaction={
        'from': owner,
    },
    kwargs={
        'totalSupply': 1000,
        'name': 'TestToken',
    },
)
contract_address = chain.wait.for_contract_address(deploy_tx_hash)
```

If I insert a `sleep(60)` or sit in the debugger until the txn has committed on Etherscan, the call succeeds.

The exception is being raised from here: https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/master/web3/manager.py#L102

Looks like `wait_for_transaction_receipt` should perhaps handle exceptions generated in web3.py?

https://github.com/ethereum/populus/blob/master/populus/utils/wait.py#L89-L95

issue_title: Could not format value as field 'extraData' on getBlock (4.0.0b11)
Release a wagon wheel to PyPI
issue_body: This is only helpful for environments where `pip` (and therefore wheel) (and currently virtualenv - see #64) is installed as they can simply install Wagon's wheel via pip and don't require any additional dependencies. Funnily enough, if they don't have `wheel` they will need a wagon of wagon and need wagon to install it :)

issue_title: Change in package specification solution
issue_body:
This issue relates to the version of `sphinx` being installed by `conda` when no specific version is required.

Running `conda create -n tmp python=3.3 numpy sphinx` suggests the following package specification solution:

```
The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    docutils:   0.12-py33_0  
    jinja2:     2.8-py33_0   
    markupsafe: 0.23-py33_0

I would have expected to see `sphinx==1.3.1` installed since it is available in the repos. In fact going through one of my older Travis CI build logs reveals that this did work as expected (i.e. `sphinx==1.3.1` was installed) on Aug 21, 2015. That Travis CI build used `conda==3.16.0`. I have tried both `conda==3.17.0` and `conda==3.16.0`. Both versions now give the above suggested package specification.

Running `conda update -n tmp --all` afterwards does not help:

issue_title: Conda meta data should support an array of licenses
issue_body: Not all software has one license.  Currently the meta data stores a string.  It should be able to store an array as well.

issue_title: UnsatisfiableError python 2.7.14 and r 3.4.1
issue_body:
I am trying to create a docker with specific version of r and python (as per our current development environment). The `Dockerfile` is as follows:

```
FROM ubuntu:14.04

RUN apt-get -qq update && apt-get -qq -y install curl bzip2 \
    && curl -sSL https://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/Miniconda2-4.3.31-Linux-x86_64.sh -o /tmp/miniconda.sh \
    && bash /tmp/miniconda.sh -bfp /usr/local \
    && rm -rf /tmp/miniconda.sh \
    && conda install -y python=2.7.14 \
    && conda update conda \
    && conda install numpy=1.13.3 scipy=1.0.0 scikit-learn=0.19.1 pandas=0.21.1 statsmodels=0.8.0 \
    && conda install r=3.4.1 r-bcp rpy2 \
    && apt-get -qq -y remove curl bzip2 \
    && apt-get -qq -y autoremove \
    && apt-get autoclean \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* /var/log/dpkg.log \
    && conda clean --all --yes

ENV PATH /opt/conda/bin:$PATH

issue_title: Windows version of conda points to nonexistent repo
issue_body:
When I try to update conda on a fresh install, I get this:

```
$ conda update conda
Fetching package metadata: .....Error: Could not find URL: https://github.com/conda/conda-recipes/win-64/
```
And https://github.com/conda/conda-recipes/win-64/ doesn't exist. What can I do?

issue_title: Specific Conda-environment permanently hangs upon activation, seemingly out of random

issue_title: broke conda when I uninstalled cryptography package
issue_body:
I was having trouble installing the pillow package and a search here seemed to indicate I may have success if I uninstalled the cryptography package and reinstalled it at version 1.1. Neither operation proceeded cleanly and when I tried to run conda to get back to where I started I got the following:

issue_title: Non-versatile shebang usage
issue_body:
Hello,

I tried installing Minoconda on Mac using `sudo ~/Downloads/Miniconda-latest-MacOSX-x86_64.sh`, but turned out shebang is hard-coded, so user need to explicitly invoke the command prefixed with `bash`.

I would have sent a PR, but I could not locate the install script in the repo.

The fix is to replace:

```sh
#!/bin/bash
```

with `env` based path:

```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
```

in all `.sh` files, which would support multiple directory structures and work on most, if not all, Unix-like operating systems.

Thanks in anticipation!

issue_title: Can we help with syntax for build numbers in CLI package specs?
issue_body:
I just made this mistake, but I think it is one many people may make:

Asked for build 0, got build 4:

```
$ conda create -n test mpc=1.0.3-0 gmp
Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

  environment location: /Users/ijstokes/anaconda/envs/test

  added / updated specs: 
    - gmp
    - mpc=1.0.3-0


The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    gmp:  6.1.2-0 conda-forge
    mpc:  1.0.3-4 conda-forge
    mpfr: 3.1.5-0 conda-forge
```

```
$ conda list -n test -e
# This file may be used to create an environment using:
# $ conda create --name <env> --file <this file>
# platform: osx-64
gmp=6.1.2=0
mpc=1.0.3=4
mpfr=3.1.5=0
```

An expert conda user (or perhaps developer!) would figure this out pretty quickly, and the output of `conda list -e` gave me a hint (but only after I started reporting a conda bug that things had gone awry), the mistake was that the build number in the spec needed to be separated with a `=` instead of a `-`.  Making the change resulted in:

```
$ conda create -n test mpc=1.0.3=0 gmp
Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

  environment location: /Users/ijstokes/anaconda/envs/test

  added / updated specs: 
    - gmp
    - mpc==1.0.3=0


The following packages will be downloaded:

    package                    |            build
    ---------------------------|-----------------
    mpc-1.0.3                  |                0          91 KB  conda-forge

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    gmp:  6.1.2-0 conda-forge
    mpc:  1.0.3-0 conda-forge
    mpfr: 3.1.5-0 conda-forge
```

which is what I wanted.  But I'd suggest the typical user probably won't pick up this nuance.  Is there anything we can do to improve the handling or at least provide a warning regarding the package spec?

issue_title: Package type
issue_body:
We have a problem, which will be amplified quite a bit when the R support gets fleshed out, that there are naming conflicts between different packages from different languages. We already see this a little bit with `lxml`.  I'll use `bokeh` as an example, because we are about to have both Python and R bindings for it.

The solution I've been using so far with R is to prefix all R package names with `r-`, but @kriehl pointed out that many R users aren't going to like this, or will be confused by it. 

My idea (and I'm not sure how this would be implemented yet) would be for each package to optionally have an associated "type". Two packages with the same name can coexist, if they have different types. Whenever a package name is used ambiguously on the command line, conda would ask which type you meant. You could also specify the type using some syntax (like say `r:bokeh`). You could also specify some configuration value to always prefer some given type (like say if you only use Python and don't care about R).

Note that this shouldn't be confused with https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/155, which is a completely different feature.

issue_title: When lacking permissions to write, clone message should quote prefix.
issue_body:
When trying to install a new package into a location that the user lacks write permissions (read-only root), conda helpfully suggests cloning the environment into a new location:

```
CondaIOError: IO error: Missing write permissions in: C:\Program Files\ArcGIS\Pro\bin\Python
#
# You don't appear to have the necessary permissions to install packages
# into the install area 'C:\Program Files\ArcGIS\Pro\bin\Python'.
# However you can clone this environment into your home directory and
# then make changes to it.
# This may be done using the command:
#
# $ conda create -n my_deathstar --clone=C:\Program Files\Anaconda\envs\deathstar
```
As shown in the example above, this clone path may include spaces. This will be particularly common on Windows, where a global install will result in files written to Program Files, which a non-administrator user will not be able to write to. Because the command presents a prefix, it should be quoted to guard against this case.

issue_title: Explain relocation support for non-relocatable code
issue_body:
I watched the video at http://conda.pydata.org/docs/ and I noticed the claim that Conda does various things about relocating code, but other than mentioning shebangs, not much is said.  Conda seems to depend strongly on all pkgs being relocatable, so this is an important subject.

Because of things like autoconf, a great number of open source codebases are non-relocatable out of the box.  Absolute install paths can be encoded in a large number of places:

 - ELF objects, in rpath/runpath
 - shebangs
 - in object code (for example, valgrind execs a helper program using the absolute install path)
 - in data files

Fixing all of these is hard, as the number of ways in which an absolute install path could be hardcoded at build-time is effectively infinite.

The rpath/runpath case can be addressed with `patchelf(1)` using `$ORIGIN`-relative paths.  Shebangs can be addressed with `sed(1)`.  The rest looks harder to me.  `LD_PRELOAD` can be used to some degree (but not on all platforms) to intercept references to build-time hardcoded install paths and rewrite them to run-time relative install paths.

So I'm curious as to what all Conda does to address this.  Perhaps I've missed something.  (I looked a bit in the sources, but nothing stuck out.)

(It really is a terrible thing that autoconf makes it so easy to write non-relocatable code...)

issue_title: how to modify conda 'source activate' ps1 behavior
issue_body:
my current bash ps1 is as follows:

```
bldred='\e[1;31m' # Red
bldcyn='\e[1;36m' # Cyan
bldwht='\e[1;37m' # White
txtrst='\e[0m'    # Text Reset - Useful for avoiding color bleed

export PS1="\n\[$bldred\]\u\[$txtrst\]@\[$bldwht\]\h\[$txtrst\]:\[$bldcyn\]\w\[$txtrst\]$ "
```

However, running:
```
source activate <env-name-here>
```

by default, tells conda to prepend the env-name to my PS1:

```
(<env-name-here>)
user@short-domain:fullpath$
```

Is there a way to tell conda to insert the env-name within my PS1 instead, specifically, right after the newline?

[stackoverflow post for this issue](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/42481726/how-to-modify-conda-env-ps1-behavior)

issue_title: After activating an environment `libselinux.so.1: cannot open shared object file`
issue_body:
I have an `environment.yml` file as pasted bellow. When I create an environment from it, and activate it, I see some weird bugs:

```bash
(myenv) $ vim
vim: error while loading shared libraries: libselinux.so.1: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory

(myenv) $ /bin/python
Could not find platform independent libraries <prefix>
Could not find platform dependent libraries <exec_prefix>
Consider setting $PYTHONHOME to <prefix>[:<exec_prefix>]
Fatal Python error: Py_Initialize: Unable to get the locale encoding
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'encodings'

Current thread 0x00007f30a8f08700 (most recent call first):
[1]    7891 abort (core dumped)  /bin/python
```

I have no idea if they are related, but it never happens when I'm not in this conda environment, so I guess they are. Notice also that I'm not using any alternative channels.

Good chancces that this is a bug in a specific package I use, and will be happy to get some hints for how to check it. However, I believe that it also related to conda, because I can create an empty environment, activate it, update with the environment file, and the bugs disappear:

```bash
$ conda create -n myenv python=3.6
...
$ source activate myenv
(myenv) $ vim  # works fine
(myenv) $ /bin/python  # works fine
(myenv) $ conda env update -f environment.yml
(myenv) $ vim  # still works fine
(myenv) $ /bin/python  # still works fine
(myenv) $ source deactivate
$ source activate myenv
(myenv) $ vim  # broken again
(myenv) $ /bin/python  # broken again
```

## Software

conda: 4.3.16
os: Arch linux 4.11.7-1
gvim: 8.0
system python: 3.6.1

## Notes

- I failed to reproduce it on another machine (conda 4.3.16, CentOS, vim 7.4.160, system python 2.7.5).
- Seems like the same issue as [here](https://stackoverflow.com/q/40110207/1224456), but due to the above I really don't think that it should be solved by system wide changes.

issue_title: Feature Request: Add ToDo markers
issue_body:
### Description

ToDo markers are often use to mark parts in the code that needs additional work later or mark very special code parts.

It would be nice to have these available in the log pane (new TODO tab).

They are always part of a comment (which is language dependent), How ToDo-markers look like likely differs from programmer to programmer / project rule, therefore it would be good to be able to set the regex for them, maybe even have different types that can be grouped in the tab, but this can come later.

Maybe the feauture already exists in OCIDE and/or HE (in this case: how should the markers look like)?

issue_title: [API Users] Can't run set_role with no value
issue_body:
You can't run the CLI command: 'username test no role', which is what is used when you 
invoke: node.api('users').set_role('test')

As an aside, this missed testing because the function names weren't prefixed with test_ so python's unittest never ran them.

The faulty code is:
```
        cmd = 'username %s' % name
        if value is not None:
            cmd += ' role %s' % value
        else:
            cmd += ' no role'
        return self.configure(cmd)
```

This works:
```
        cmd = 'username %s' % name
        if value is not None:
            cmd += ' role %s' % value
        else:
            cmd = 'default username %s role' % name
        return self.configure(cmd)
```

issue_title: Proposition for an additional constant parameter in check_stable function
issue_body:
The current behaviour for check_stable is that : "If a second window is started before the previous is completed the second start event will be ignored and the window will be completed by the next end event."

Would it be good to consider an addition constant called _Allow_StartEvent_To_Restart_ where if this is set to true, then upon the assertion of a new start_event, to reset the stability check?

There are some cases where I find it inconvenient to trigger end_event explicitly. for example if the start and end event are derived from the same signal source.

```python
from sqlalchemy import *
from cockroachdb.sqlalchemy import run_transaction
import sqlalchemy.orm

                'title_dict': [

    else:

def cockroachdb_populate_dicts(engine):
    metadata = MetaData(engine)
    for tname in dict_entries:
        sessionmaker = sqlalchemy.orm.sessionmaker(engine)
        ins = Table(tname, metadata, autoload=True).insert()
        def callback(session):

                session.add(item)

    with engine.begin() as connection:

issue_title: [Discussion] Could the help text for the VNet create command be a little clearer?
issue_body:
Could the behavior of the `az network vnet create` command's `--subnet-prefix` parameter be clarified any further? It's help text states that it will reserve a portion of the vnet's address space, but in my experience, it seems to use the following specific heuristic: `<vnet-prefix>/max(24, <vnet-cidr-block>)`.

Maybe the behavior isn't this simple, but if it is, it would be cool if the help text stated this, that way a user would know exactly when they do or don't need to explicitly set it (e.g. `If omitted, automatically reserves a `/24` (or as large as available) block within the VNet address space, .`).

This isn't that high-pri, and maybe I'm just being nit-picky, but I had to try the `az network vnet create` command with and without the `--subnet-prefix` parameter in order to see how it worked.

issue_title: Configuration object creation (similar to powershell)
issue_body:
There is small but growing divide the between capability of AZ CLI and Azure Powershell.

Of particular note is object creation in Powershell. In Powershell, a complete configuration object can be created using multiple commands. [Example ($gw)](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/application-gateway/application-gateway-configure-redirect-powershell):
```
# Get the application gateway
$gw = Get-AzureRmApplicationGateway -Name AdatumAppGateway -ResourceGroupName AdatumAppGatewayRG

# Get the existing HTTPS listener
$httpslistener = Get-AzureRmApplicationGatewayHttpListener -Name appgatewayhttplistener -ApplicationGateway $gw

# Get the existing front end IP configuration
$fipconfig = Get-AzureRmApplicationGatewayFrontendIPConfig -Name appgatewayfrontendip -ApplicationGateway $gw

# Add a new front end port to support HTTP traffic
Add-AzureRmApplicationGatewayFrontendPort -Name appGatewayFrontendPort2  -Port 80 -ApplicationGateway $gw

# Get the recently created port
$fp = Get-AzureRmApplicationGatewayFrontendPort -Name appGatewayFrontendPort2 -ApplicationGateway $gw

# Create a new HTTP listener using the port created earlier
Add-AzureRmApplicationGatewayHttpListener -Name appgatewayhttplistener2  -Protocol Http -FrontendPort $fp -FrontendIPConfiguration $fipconfig -ApplicationGateway $gw 

# Get the new listener
$listener = Get-AzureRmApplicationGatewayHttpListener -Name appgatewayhttplistener2 -ApplicationGateway $gw

# Add a redirection configuration using a permanent redirect and targeting the existing listener
Add-AzureRmApplicationGatewayRedirectConfiguration -Name redirectHttptoHttps -RedirectType Permanent -TargetListener $httpslistener -IncludePath $true -IncludeQueryString $true -ApplicationGateway $gw

# Get the redirect configuration
$redirectconfig = Get-AzureRmApplicationGatewayRedirectConfiguration -Name redirectHttptoHttps -ApplicationGateway $gw


# Add a new rule to handle the redirect and use the new listener
Add-AzureRmApplicationGatewayRequestRoutingRule -Name rule02 -RuleType Basic -HttpListener $listener -RedirectConfiguration $redirectconfig -ApplicationGateway $gw

# Update the application gateway
Set-AzureRmApplicationGateway -ApplicationGateway $gw 
```

**Why does this matter/needed?**

Application Gateway can take a whole day(!) to deploy due to each individual change taking anywhere from [5-45 minutes to apply](https://feedback.azure.com/forums/281804-azure-resource-manager/suggestions/19119910-application-gateway-management-operations-are-agon).

Powershell avoids this generating the configuration object and then applying it in bulk to the Application Gateway, resulting in only 1 operation.

Presumably this will continue to be the case with more and more PaaS offerings in Azure. Is the right answer here to build this capability in AZ CLI or perhaps the right answer is to improve mgmt PaaS offerings that are so cludgy? This would be a suggestion for the former, as to some extent understand why Application Gateway takes some time to apply changes (multiple nodes, etc).

issue_title: Unhelpful traceback produced if peering vnet name is specified instead of resource id
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
When creating a vnet peer and specifying a vnet name instead of full resource id, an unhelpful traceback is produced. Help output is also not sufficiently clear that the input is a full path/resource ID instead of just the vnet name.

**To Reproduce**
Command:
```
az network vnet peering create  --name hub-to-spoke1 \
  --resource-group spoke2 --vnet-name spoke2-vnet \
  --remote-vnet-id hub-vnet \
  --allow-vnet-access --allow-forwarded-traffic --allow-gateway-transit
```

**Actual behavior**
```
'subscription'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/azure-cli/2.0.43/libexec/lib/python3.7/site-packages/knack/cli.py", line 197, in invoke
    cmd_result = self.invocation.execute(args)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/azure-cli/2.0.43/libexec/lib/python3.7/site-packages/azure/cli/core/commands/__init__.py", line 369, in execute
    six.reraise(*sys.exc_info())
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/azure-cli/2.0.43/libexec/lib/python3.7/site-packages/six.py", line 693, in reraise
    raise value
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/azure-cli/2.0.43/libexec/lib/python3.7/site-packages/azure/cli/core/commands/__init__.py", line 343, in execute
    result = cmd(params)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/azure-cli/2.0.43/libexec/lib/python3.7/site-packages/azure/cli/core/commands/__init__.py", line 182, in __call__
    return self.handler(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/azure-cli/2.0.43/libexec/lib/python3.7/site-packages/azure/cli/core/__init__.py", line 436, in default_command_handler
    result = op(**command_args)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/azure-cli/2.0.43/libexec/lib/python3.7/site-packages/azure/cli/command_modules/network/custom.py", line 2938, in create_vnet_peering
    aux_subscription = parse_resource_id(remote_virtual_network)['subscription']
KeyError: 'subscription'
```

**Expected behavior**
Simple error noting that an invalid resource id (e.g. `/subscriptions/...`) was not provided, and that the name alone is not sufficient.

Specifically: when `parse_resource_id` cannot parse the resource it should raise an error, and this error should be caught by the command handler to produce a better actionable error message.

issue_title: Feature: download and upload blob in batch.
issue_body:
this is a proposal of extending the blob download and upload command to support move files in batch.

Download files from one container recursively:
``` bash
# reference the source by URL
az storage blob download -s https://sample.blob.core.windows.net/sample01/ -d ./local

# reference the source by container name, account name need to be given explicitly
az storage blob download -s sample01 -d ./local --account-name sample

# select files by pattern
az storage blob download -s https://sample.blob.core.windows.net/sample01/ -d ./local --pattern *.json
```

Upload files from local folder to remote container recursively:
``` bash
# reference the destination by URL, this command will recursively upload all files and keep the sample directory structure
az storage blob upload -s ./local -d https://sample.blob.core.windows/sample1

# reference the destination by container name
az storage blob upload -s ./local -d sample1  --account-name sample

# upload recursively select file by pattern
az storage blob upload -s ./local -d sample1 --account-name --pattern *.json
```

issue_title: Unsuitable behaviour for reading parts of files
issue_body:
### Description

When using the package to access the Azure Data Lake Store to read Apache Parquet files with `pyarrow`, it can be seen that we retrieve significantly more data than needed on selective reads. Using columnar file formats like Apache Parquet are essentials for analytic access as the engine reading these files can already push parts of the query down into the storage layer. Data that would be pruned by the engine is not even retrieved from the storage.

As an example, we read for an example 50MB Parquet file the *last* 65536 bytes to load the metadata. This is then cached in RAM in an `AzureDLFile`  object. As a follow-up to this, `pyarrow` now requests the relevant columns, starting with the first in the file. These columns are in roughly 8MB chunks which would be suitable to be loaded in independent requests. But instead of only loading the relevant data, the code in https://github.com/Azure/azure-data-lake-store-python/blob/master/azure/datalake/store/core.py#L768-L773 will load the data from the beginning of the first relevant column until the start of the previous request. In most cases, this request spans 95% of the file and so subsequent requests are then done as the data already resides in memory.

For columnar reads, this situation is unfortunate as we have transferred nearly all data but for the successful read of the file, we may only need 10% of the file. In the usual case, such selective reads are not wanted as they increase the number of requests heavily but for formats like Parquet they are essential to take full benefit of the format. For comparison, the S3 implementation in the newest Hadoop version let's the user select different input policies: https://hadoop.apache.org/docs/current/hadoop-aws/tools/hadoop-aws/index.html#S3A_Experimental_fadvise_input_policy_support In most of the cases, one would chose `sequential` here but for analytics reads/queries, `random` gives a much better performance.

---

### Reproduction Steps

```
from azure.datalake.store import lib
from azure.datalake.store.core import AzureDLFileSystem

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import pyarrow as pa
import pyarrow.parquet as pq


# Add secrets and store_name …
token = lib.auth(tenant_id, client_id=client_id, client_secret=client_secret)
adl = AzureDLFileSystem(token, store_name=store_name)

size = 1000000

df = pd.DataFrame({"col1": np.random.randint(0, 100, size=size),
                   "col2": np.random.randint(0, 100, size=size),
                   "col3": np.random.randn(size),
                   "col4": np.random.randn(size),
                   "col5": np.random.randn(size),
                   "col6": np.random.randn(size),
                   "col7": np.random.randn(size),
                   "col8": np.random.randn(size),
                   "col9": np.random.randn(size),
                   "col10": np.random.randn(size)})

table = pa.Table.from_pandas(df)
filename = 'test.parquet'
with adl.open(filename, 'wb') as f:
    pq.write_table(table, f)

# This should only read a small selection of the file, currently it reads about 90% 
with adl.open(filename, 'rb') as f:
    table2 = pq.read_table(f, columns=['col2', 'col7'])
```

### Environment summary

**SDK Version:** 0.0.17

**Python Version:** 64bit, Python 3.6

**OS Version:** macOS Sierra

**Shell Type:** zsh

issue_title: Django rest framework doesn't use has_object_create_permission
issue_body:
I saw in the tests `has_object_create_permission`. I tried this method in my API and found it isn't called, because there is no object when creating an object. I read the Django REST framework docs and saw that object-level permissions are only called when `get_object()` is called, which isn't the case when creating a new object.

Ref. http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/permissions/

Two things: clarify the test and update the docs?

issue_title: Hook contribution & minor suggestions
issue_body:
Hello,

I'd like to introduce you to 2 simple pre-commit hook I wrote:
https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks

It felt really simple to write, cleanly architectured and easy to share with colleagues.
So thanks a lot for your work on this !

Now, I have some minor complains:

- **no support for local hook development** : I had to create an initial github repo and then hack my changes in `$HOME/.pre-commit/repoXXXXXX/py_env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pre_commit_hooks/forbid_crlf.py` while working on it. There should be an easier way to do this !

- the **required sha** property in `.pre-commit-config.yaml` : combined to the pain point #-1 above, it was really annoying to have to update it each time I was committing a new version of my hook on github. Couldn't you defined **a default empty value meaning to track the lastest version** ? I know I could use tags to specify stable versions, but it doesn't fit a work-in-progress development situation.

- the lack of instructions on how to develop a hook: your docs **never mention the necessity to have a `setup.py` file with entry points defined**, while you actually need it as a `virtualenv` is used under the hood. My [commit history](https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks/commits/master) illustrates how puzzling developping a hook can become :D

What's your take on all of those issues, and which features would you be ok to integrate given that someone would suggest a pull request ?

And again, thank you very much for this awesome hooks manager !

issue_title: Cflags in bullet.pc are incorrect when built with CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX
issue_body:
`bullet.pc.in` contains 

```
 Cflags: @BULLET_DOUBLE_DEF@ -I@CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX@/@INCLUDE_INSTALL_DIR@ -I@CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX@/include
```

while `BulletConfig.cmake.in` contains

```
set ( BULLET_INCLUDE_DIR  "@INCLUDE_INSTALL_DIR@" )
set ( BULLET_INCLUDE_DIRS "@INCLUDE_INSTALL_DIR@" )
```

when building with the following flags taken from the openSUSE build

```
/usr/bin/cmake .. -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX:PATH=/usr -DINCLUDE_INSTALL_DIR:PATH=/usr/include -DLIB_INSTALL_DIR:PATH=/usr/lib64 -DSYSCONF_INSTALL_DIR:PATH=/etc -DSHARE_INSTALL_PREFIX:PATH=/usr/share -DCMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR:PATH=/usr/lib64 -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=None '-DCMAKE_C_FLAGS=-fmessage-length=0 -grecord-gcc-switches -O2 -Wall -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 -fstack-protector -funwind-tables -fasynchronous-unwind-tables -DNDEBUG' '-DCMAKE_CXX_FLAGS=-fmessage-length=0 -grecord-gcc-switches -O2 -Wall -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 -fstack-protector -funwind-tables -fasynchronous-unwind-tables -DNDEBUG' '-DCMAKE_Fortran_FLAGS=-fmessage-length=0 -grecord-gcc-switches -O2 -Wall -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 -fstack-protector -funwind-tables -fasynchronous-unwind-tables -DNDEBUG' '-DCMAKE_EXE_LINKER_FLAGS=-Wl,--as-needed -Wl,--no-undefined -Wl,-z,now' '-DCMAKE_MODULE_LINKER_FLAGS=-Wl,--as-needed -Wl,--no-undefined -Wl,-z,now' '-DCMAKE_SHARED_LINKER_FLAGS=-Wl,--as-needed -Wl,--no-undefined -Wl,-z,now' -DLIB_SUFFIX=64 -DCMAKE_SKIP_RPATH:BOOL=ON -DCMAKE_VERBOSE_MAKEFILE:BOOL=ON -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS:BOOL=ON -DBUILD_STATIC_LIBS:BOOL=OFF -DCMAKE_COLOR_MAKEFILE:BOOL=OFF -DCMAKE_INSTALL_DO_STRIP:BOOL=OFF -DCMAKE_MODULES_INSTALL_DIR=/usr/share/cmake/Modules -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=RelWithDebInfo -DINCLUDE_INSTALL_DIR=/usr/include/bullet -DBUILD_BULLET2_DEMOS=OFF -DBUILD_OPENGL3_DEMOS=OFF
```

Particularly the combination of `-DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX:PATH=/usr` and `-DINCLUDE_INSTALL_DIR=/usr/include/bullet` this leads to the CFlags in the bullet.pc file being `/usr/usr/include/bullet`.
To be consistent with what is in the  `BulletConfig.cmake.in` file `bullet.pc.in` should contain `Cflags: @BULLET_DOUBLE_DEF@ -I@INCLUDE_INSTALL_DIR@ -I@CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX@/include` Instead

Note that cmake is called by a RPM macro hence the extra useless flags and make install is called as `/usr/bin/make VERBOSE=1 DESTDIR=/home/abuild/rpmbuild/BUILDROOT/libbullet-2.83.7-8.1.x86_64 install/fast -C build`

issue_title: Extract rotation and diagonlize do not work.
issue_body:
Example inertia tensor:
0.00076275 -0.00047298 0
-0.00047298 0.0019185 0
0 0 0.0022293

btMatrix3x3 inertia(0.00076275, -0.00047298, 0, -0.00047298, 0.0019185, 0, 0, 0, 0.0022293);
btQuaternion q = btQuaternion::getIdentity();
inertia.extractRotation(q);

The omega computed in extractRotation is 0 on the very first iteration of the loop resulting in exiting. This basically just returns your identity quaternion.... This function has been in bullet for a long time I'm hoping I'm just doing something wrong but...

Omega computes the sum of cross product of the columns of the identity q and the inertia tensor. This result in 0,0,0 since you get 0,0,-0.4730
+ 0,0,0.4730 and 0,0,0

Since extract rotation doesn't work, neither does diagonlize.

Running eig to do vd decomp in matlab reveals correct rotation and principle axis. Most probably won't notice that these things are broken since their code is probably just ripping the diagonal from the inertia matrix and using identity for the rotation to principle axis.

issue_title: web.View didn't support multi inheritance init chain
issue_body:
## Long story short

I am using class based view, when creating my own base handler class inherited from web.View and other CommonMixin class,  the ``__init__`` chain call broken due to web.View and abc.AbstractView didn't use ``super().__init__(request)``

```python3
from aiohttp.web import View

class CommonHandler(object):
    def __init__(self, req):
         super().__init__()
         print('common init done')

class BaseHandler(View, CommonHandler):
    def __init__(self, req):
        super().__init__(req)

hdl = BaseHandler(None)
```
## Expected behaviour
output `common init done`


## Actual behaviour
no output, CommonHandler.__init__() didn't called.

## Steps to reproduce

above code

## Your environment
linux
python 3.5.2
aiohttp.__version__ 1.3.3

I used this patch to fix it:
```diff
diff --git a/aiohttp/abc.py b/aiohttp/abc.py
index dc34325..256f3c7 100644
--- a/aiohttp/abc.py
+++ b/aiohttp/abc.py
@@ -81,6 +81,7 @@ class AbstractMatchInfo(ABC):
 class AbstractView(ABC):
 
     def __init__(self, request):
+        super().__init__(request)
         self._request = request
 
     @property
diff --git a/aiohttp/web_urldispatcher.py b/aiohttp/web_urldispatcher.py
index c8b0519..b78c6cb 100644
--- a/aiohttp/web_urldispatcher.py
+++ b/aiohttp/web_urldispatcher.py
@@ -646,6 +646,9 @@ class SystemRoute(AbstractRoute):
 
 class View(AbstractView):
 
+    def __init__(self, request):
+        super().__init__(request)
+
     @asyncio.coroutine
     def __iter__(self):
         if self.request._method not in hdrs.METH_ALL:
```

issue_title: Run cells before notebook is live
issue_body:
When a notebook just got opened, and as the kernel is still starting, it is already possible to "execute" the first cells with `Shift+Enter`.

However, execution requests are lost instead of being queued and the later cells executed likely error due to the missing first cells. This problem is especially visible with windows where the kernel startup time is longer than on OS X or linux. 

We should probably either queue the messages or keep the current cell selected.

issue_title: List take not compatible with slices
issue_body:
Since the recent revisions to the core, container types don't seem to be compatible with taking a slice (as opposed to just a single element). As a minimal example:
```python
>>> from autograd import grad
>>> def f(x):
...     s = slice(None, -1, None)
...     y = x[s]
...     return y[0]
... 
>>> grad(f)([1., 2., 3.])
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/jamietownsend/autograd/autograd/convenience_wrappers.py", line 26, in gradfun
    return vjp(1.0)
  File "/Users/jamietownsend/autograd/autograd/core.py", line 18, in <lambda>
    return lambda g : backward_pass(g, end_node, start_node), end_node
  File "/Users/jamietownsend/autograd/autograd/core.py", line 37, in backward_pass
    cur_outgrad = vsum(node.vspace, *outgrads[node])
  File "/Users/jamietownsend/autograd/autograd/core.py", line 131, in vsum
    return primitive_vsum(vspace, *args)
  File "/Users/jamietownsend/autograd/autograd/core.py", line 70, in __call__
    result_value = self.fun(*argvals, **kwargs)
  File "/Users/jamietownsend/autograd/autograd/core.py", line 138, in primitive_vsum
    ans = arg.mut_add(ans)
  File "/Users/jamietownsend/autograd/autograd/container_types.py", line 30, in mut_add
    result[idx] = vs.shape[idx].mut_add(result[idx], x)
AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'mut_add'
```
Anything where you take more than one list element in one go will break, e.g.
```python
>>> def f(x):
...     y = x[1:3]
...     return y[0]
... 
>>> grad(f)([1., 2., 3.])
```
This breaks the varational_autoencoder example (which is how I found the bug). In particular line 48:
```python
    for W, b in params[:-1]:
```
I can try to work out a fix for this but thought I would raise it in case one of you guys knows how to fix it real quick 😉.

issue_title: Server core dumps if bitcoind isn't reachable
issue_body:
Currently `blockstack-server start` and `blockstack-server stop` result in a core dump (Ubuntu 16.04.1 LTS) if `bitcoind` isn't running (or unreachable).

This makes it more complicated than necessarily to start `bitcoind` and `blockstack-server` on the same machine. You have to wait until `bitcoind` starts up and its RPC api is accessible before starting blockstack-server. I accomplish this with a script that sleeps 30 seconds.

issue_title: Allow selection of the coverage report filename
issue_body:
coverage.py allows for one to choose the filename (defaulting to .coverage). Because of the regexes, some of the possible variations in filenames are not detected (in a project that I recently worked on, the format `.coverage.anywordhere`) was not detected (coverage.py allows variable substitution from the os environment in its configuration and anywordhere was being substituted as either unit or functional depending on the build, for context).

While I've solved my own problem by reordering the format in the config to be `${ENV_VAR}.coverage`; which is just as fine to me, others may want some other naming scheme for a variety of purposes.

A simple CLI argument specifying which report to collect and upload would suffice (this would run instead of the collect reports section)

issue_title: document implprefix argument?
issue_body:
Hi,
I'm new to the project and I'm considering using pluggy in my own application.
So I started by looking at pluggy's documentation, as well as the way it's being used by pytest.

While looking at the latter, I noticed that the `PluginManager` can also take a `implprefix` argument in order to search for matching names in a plugin namespace to be registered as hook implementations.
I also noticed pytest plugins have hooks prefixed with "pytest_", and it doesn't seem necessary to mark implementations with `pytest.hookimpl` decorator to make them discoverable; the latter is of course required if one wishes to customize the behavior of a hook (by passing options to the decorator like hookwrapper, optionalhook, etc.).

As far as I can see, the main pluggy documentation at http://pluggy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ only mentions the `HookimplMarker` decorator as the way to mark a function for detection by a PluginManager's `register` method.
It's true that `implprefix` is mentioned in the "Public API" section of the docs, as it's in the PluginManager's docstring. However, maybe it could be featured more prominently in the main section?

Or is this intentional and the decorator approach is the method which should be preferred for detection of hook implementations?

Thank you

issue_title: [Feature request] add a loop fixture
issue_body:
People very often asked why `pytest-asyncio` and `pytest-aiohttp` are not compatible.
Well, AFAIK they are not mutually exclusive but the compatibility is not perfect still.

I had very strong objection for `pytest-asyncio` behavior: from my perspective `event_loop` fixture should call `asyncio.set_event_loop(None)` before running a test. It's prevent forgetting to pass `loop` param to calls like `asyncio.sleep()` etc.

But not I withdraw my objection: since Python 3.5.3 the coroutine always gets a running event loop regardless to `asyncio.set_event_loop()` param.

We still have many differences between `pytest-asyncio` and `pytest-aiohttp`: a need for special `@pytest.mark.asyncio` markup for `pytest-asyncio` and event loop naming.

In `pytest-asyncio` it is `event_loop` but int `pytest-aiohttp` the name is `loop`.

The `loop` name is used everywhere in `asyncio` documentation, I love to keep it (at least the name is shorter).

@Tinche would you consider adding the `loop` fixture as an alias for `event_loop`?

I'm not a `pytest` jedi, in my mind the fixture should look like:

```
@pytest.yield_fixture
def loop(event_loop):
    return event_loop
```

but maybe you have a better idea.

`django-widget-tweaks` is listed in https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/cd5bb8f741ec68a7f29b3c7379defd56d4774068/setup.py#L70 so it's not immediately obvious to me why make install doesn't install it.

issue_title: .Net Core Class Library and Associated Settings
issue_body:
I am starting to work with .Net core and i am struggling to follow tutorials etc because it seems as though the framework has moved on even though some of the tutorials a just a few months old.  One area were i am completely befuddled is class libraries but i am not sure if its a language misunderstanding, and tooling one or a bit of both.

# Class Libraries

I have downloaded VS2017 RC (Community) to start working on this and i want to create a class library to contain data access logic for example, but visual studio **only** allows me to create a .Net Standard class library, so i am not sure if my VS install is busted and i should get both (Core and Standard Class Library Templates).

The Standard Template (which at first i thought meant ASP.Net not Core)  i now understand to be the cross platform setup.  Issue i have is it doesn't seem to correlate to any examples that i have seen, i.e. it has a dependencies item in the file-list but does not contain a projects.json for example, which pretty much all examples show as a route to manage dependencies, tooling etc.

What am i missing here, has the .Net Core class library been superseded by the .Net standard class library, does the .Net standard library not require a projects.json or should i create one anyway? and is my templates install busted on visual studio 2017?

I really love the direction this is going, but my brain can't keep up with the switch in language/names (Core, Standard etc) and choices that occur in such a short amount of time and i would really appreciate some clarity on what i should be utilising moving forwards.

# Associated Settings for Class Libraries

I can see some threads on here already pertaining to the demise of the app.config, but how could we create a class library that is self contained with regards to settings, do we, as i have seen some examples of have to turn it into an "App" of some sort so we can access to the Setup classes/Methods (via a program.cs for example) to be able read in a configuration file for that specific class library, is that the approach at the moment?

issue_title: Process pipes aren't garbage collected
issue_body:
# Process pipes aren't garbage collected
# General
When using redirection for StandardOutput (and possibly StandardError, StandardInput as well) the pipes aren't garbage collected when the Process reference is collected.

The following silly piece of code lives (probably) forever in mono, but crashes in dotnet after 217 iterations on my machine:
```
public static void Main() {
            for (int i = 0; i < int.MaxValue; i++) {
                Process.Start(new ProcessStartInfo {
                    FileName = "ls",
                    UseShellExecute = false,
                    RedirectStandardOutput = true
                });
                Console.WriteLine(i);
            }
}
```
Adding a ```.StandardOutput.Close()``` extends lifetime 5 fold, indicating other things are not being collected.

issue_title: <ProjectAssetsFile> path is broken on Bash for Windows (blocks building NuGet packages)
issue_body:
# Issue Title

`<ProjectAssetsFile>` path is broken on Bash for Windows (blocks building NuGet packages)

# General

`dotnet --version`: `2.1.300`

My project builds properly on both vanilla Windows and Linux. However, the build fails on Bash for Windows:

`/usr/share/dotnet/sdk/2.1.300/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/targets/Microsoft.PackageDependencyResolution.targets(198,5): error : Assets file '/mnt/c/repos/my-repo/MyProject/C:/repos/my-repo/MyProject/obj/project.assets.json' not found. Run a NuGet package restore to generate this file. [/mnt/c/repos/my-repo/MyProject/MyProject.csproj]`

From the diagnostic build log:


    ProjectAssetsFile = /mnt/c/repos/my-repo/MyProject/obj/project.assets.json

    ...

    Property reassignment: $(ProjectAssetsFile)="/mnt/c/repos/my-repo/MyProject/C:/repos/my-repo/MyProject/obj/project.assets.json" (previous value: "C:\repos\my-repo\MyProject\obj\project.assets.json") at /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/2.1.300/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/targets/Microsoft.PackageDependencyResolution.targets (28,5)


The line in question:

`<ProjectAssetsFile>$([MSBuild]::NormalizePath($(MSBuildProjectDirectory), $(ProjectAssetsFile)))</ProjectAssetsFile>`

So somehow `ProjectAssetsFile` is being reassigned from `/mnt/c/repos/my-repo/MyProject/obj/project.assets.json` to `C:\repos\my-repo\MyProject\obj\project.assets.json` without the reassignment appearing in the diagnostic log.

This is a blocker for local builds on Windows--my repository must be built from bash for reasons unrelated to the project described above.

issue_title: Dynamically computed install_requires: investigate potential problems
issue_body:
In https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/pull/1661#discussion_r46522959 @mgedmin said:

> Hmm. Dynamically-computed install_requires causes problems for users of pip >= 7.0, which has wheel caching enabled by default: pypa/pip#3025. Short version:
> 
> - I pip install letsencrypt on Python 2.7, pip runs setup.py and builds a wheel (marked -py2-none-any.whl) and puts it in ~/.cache/pip/
> - I pip install letsencrypt on Python 2.6, pip discovers a valid wheel and installs it, using install_requires computed earlier for a different Python version."

#2175 might be related

issue_title: Don't list wildcard domains as authenticatable
issue_body:
Steps to reproduce:

In /etc/apache/sites-available/SITE, have the line:

`ServerAlias *.example.com`

Run `letsencrypt`.

Actual result:

At the "Which names would you like to activate HTTPS for?" menu, *.example.com is listed. However, if you select it, you get the error:

`Error: unauthorized :: The client lacks sufficient authorization :: Error creating new authz :: Syntax error`

Expected result:

Wildcard names should not be listed. Possibly the app should acknowledge that they exist but make them unselectable, with help text explaining that LE doesn't support wildcards.

issue_title: Hide tracebacks in the client [by default?]
issue_body:
Currently the client tends to error out with raw Python tracebacks.  We should definitely hide / provide human explanations instead of those.  Possibly we should have a way for developers to get the traceback, either via a logfile or a <tt>--debug</tt> flag or both.

@bradmw is going to work on this, I believe.

issue_title: Determine *-auto directory layout
issue_body:
Unfortunately, the way that GitHub handles symlinks isn't ideal for `*-auto`. For example, here's a [symlink to a directory](https://github.com/certbot/testrepo2/blob/master/amul2). Symlinks to files are treated the same way. The raw contents of the symlink is simply the target's path, not the contents.

With this in mind I propose we leave `letsencrypt-auto-source` largely unmodified. Moving this directory or making it a symlink to `certbot-auto-source` will break `letsencrypt-auto`. I also think keeping both `letsencrypt-auto` and `certbot-auto` in the `letsencrypt-auto-source` directory is unnecessary as they will be the same file. This means both `certbot-auto` and `letsencrypt-auto` will be downloading `letsencrypt-auto-source/letsencrypt-auto` for updates, but this is an implementation detail and not a big deal in my opinion.

The other question we have to consider is about `*-auto`s in the root of our repo. `letsencrypt-auto` must remain in our repo in some form. All of our installation instructions use it and have done so since the beginning of public beta.

Do we want a `certbot-auto` in the root of our repo? I think we do as having `letsencrypt-auto` be the only `-auto` script in our repo when `certbot-auto` is the preferred form is a bit awkward. We could make one of the scripts a symlink to the other. The problem is the breaks anyone doing `wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/master/letsencrypt-auto` (or with `certbot-auto`). While I haven't seen anyone do this, we can avoid this potential problem by having full copies of both `certbot-auto` and `letsencrypt-auto` in the root directory. I think I prefer this approach over using symlinks.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Remove references to TUF
issue_body:
**Description of issue or feature request**:

`securesystemslib` was not always a library. Many of its modules and functions previously lived in the [TUF repository](https://github.com/theupdateframework/tuf).  Unfortunately, there are still references to TUF in some of the `securesystemslib` comments.

**Current behavior**:
There are references to TUF in comments and there exist schemata that are only relevant to TUF.  For example:
https://github.com/secure-systems-lab/securesystemslib/blob/d1116044cfd0a9fd04077ececef9f8c148136f25/securesystemslib/formats.py#L18-L24

https://github.com/secure-systems-lab/securesystemslib/blob/d1116044cfd0a9fd04077ececef9f8c148136f25/securesystemslib/formats.py#L326-L334

**Expected behavior**:
TUF references in `securesystemslib` should be removed and TUF-related schemata relocated to the TUF repository.

issue_title: Add some locale-aware test skipping to tests
issue_body:
I have not yet been able to reliably create a Windows VM with a non-US timezone locale for whatever reason (much less in Appveyor), but I suspect that in non-US locales, there may be issues running tests like [this one](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/test/test_tz.py#L408) and [this one](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/test/test_tz.py#L476), which use English-language time zone names.

Using the [tzwinbase.list()](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/tz/win.py#L155) functionality, we can probably detect whether or not those time zones are available, and skip otherwise. We may also want to add in some logic to check the actual locale, and add in a test that fails if a few of those aren't in the `list()` functionality in a US / English locale.

issue_title: TZID is not supported in rrule DTSTART
issue_body:
```python
from dateutil import rrule
rrule.rrulestr('DTSTART;TZID=US-Eastern:20180119T130000Z RRULE:FREQ=DAILY;INTERVAL=1;COUNT=7')
```

```python

  File "/Users/ryan/venvs/tmp-c74b801dfc74a8d7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/dateutil/rrule.py", line 1569, in _parse_rfc
    raise ValueError("unsupported DTSTART parm: "+parm)
```

From https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5545.txt:

>       The following are examples of this property parameter:
> 
>        DTSTART;TZID=America/New_York:19980119T020000
> 
>        DTEND;TZID=America/New_York:19980119T030000
> 
>       The "TZID" property parameter MUST NOT be applied to DATE
>       properties and DATE-TIME or TIME properties whose time values are
>       specified in UTC.

Is there a way to accomplish this in `dateutil` that I'm unaware of?

issue_title: Nested.default — incorrect documentation
issue_body:
`Nested` field documentation says that the `default` parameter may be used to serialize `None` values:
`    :param default: Default value to if attribute is missing or None`
However, this does not correspond to reality: `default` values are only used to serialize missing fields. And it seems that there is no way to make the `default` value be used for serialization of `None` values — by fine-tuning the field parameters, at least.

issue_title: Unknown format code 'f' for object of type 'unicode'
issue_body:
After the upgrade to `2.10.2`, our application schemas that use float and decimal value fields are breaking.

Here's a simple case that now breaks (and doesn't in `2.10.1`):

```
from marshmallow import fields, Schema


class MySchema(Schema):
    test = fields.Decimal(allow_none=True, as_string=True, places=2)

schema = MySchema()
schema.dump({})
```

issue_title: RFC: replace attribute, load_from and dump_to with a single parameter
issue_body:
See discussion in https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/pull/714.

In the general case, dump/load is symmetric and if you have a model field that is exposed with another name, you use the API name in the schema, and specify the model name as `attribute`.

```python
         class MySchema(Schema):
             ApiName = fields.String(attribute='model_name')
```

`dump_to` and `load_from` introduce asymmetry, allowing to specify a different key for dump and load:

```python
         class MySchema(Schema):
             model_name = fields.String(load_from='api_load_name', dump_to='api_dump_name')
```

If you want to reproduce use case 1 using these, you need to specify both `load_from` and `dump_to` and make sure they match:

```python
         class MySchema(Schema):
             model_name = fields.String(load_from='api_name', dump_to='api_name')
```

The flexibility brought by having both `load_from` and `dump_to` comes at a price: complexity for the user but also in the code (marshmallow and related libs like webargs, apispec...), potential undefined cases for some weird combinations...

Assuming symmetry is not required, a single parameter should be enough. There is however a limitation with `attribute`. It can't be used for APIs where the keys are invalid Python variable names:

```python
class MySchema(Schema):
    ApiKey = fields.String(attribute='apikey')
    weird-key = fields.String(attribute='weird_key')  # syntax error
```

Currently, `load_from` and `dump_to` can be used for this as shown above.

The proposal here is to introduce a new parameter to unite them all. Let's call it `key` for now, short of a better name:

```python
class MySchema(Schema):
    apikey = fields.Int(key='ApiKey')
    weird_key = fields.Int(key='weird-key')
```

If we do that, then there is no point in `attribute` anymore (except backward compatibility). So we'd remove `attribute`, `dump_to` and `load_from`.

Any objection to this?

Any any for a better name?

issue_title: Handle columns without `python_type`
issue_body:
To determine the field class to be used for a SQLAlchemy column, we sometimes check its `python_type`. But some columns, e.g. Postgres `TSVECTOR` columns, don't have a `python_type` and raise a `NotImplementedError` on trying to access. Some possible fixes:

* Add `TSVECTOR` to `SQLA_TYPE_MAPPING`. This would kind of fix the immediate problem I'm running into, but what's the right marshmallow type to use? We could use `Str`, but that's not exactly right. Also, this doesn't help if other columns are missing `python_type`, which might or might not be the case.
* Allow developers to exclude unusual fields from model conversion, e.g. via the `Meta.exclude` option in marshmallow. This is more flexible but requires developers to understand that they can / should exclude columns that don't convert to fields.
* Don't raise an exception on failing to convert a column to a field. Possibly add a `strict` option to `fields_for_model`, such that `ModelConversionErrors` are only raised when `strict` is true. This is also flexible, but allows developers to silence potentially heterogeneous kinds of exceptions.

I'm submitting a patch for the second option, mostly so I can get some unrelated work done, but I think the third also makes sense, and it might not be bad to use both. What do you think @sloria?

issue_title: Consider including Python unit tests in packaged wheel
issue_body:
Most open source Python packages include the test code in the wheel for distribution. There seem to be a lot of upsides to this, and only a few downsides. Upsides:

* Wheels are testable anywhere, without having to copy any other files over. Tests can be run more easily on a range of environments, just by having the wheel installed.
* Whether the test code uses package-level or relative/local imports, it'll work, since it will always be located relative to the package code where it expects to be.
* Various other scripts in our repo that move test code around and run the tests can be simplified.

Downsides:

* Would increase the size of the wheel by about 2 MB (surprisingly substantial - this includes some in-repo test data).
* Would increase the footprint of Turi Create installations.

issue_title: Related objects are not updated - Django 1.8
issue_body:
Django 1.8 disabled the assignment of unsaved foreign key values to models. This causes setattr in full_hydrate to raise a ValueError which results in the model being added to bundle.related_objects_to_save.

In save_related the FK is loaded from bundle.obj attributes since the FK is always there for our model. This causes the further logic to always use the old value of our Related object.

see: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/ref/models/fields/#django.db.models.ForeignKey.allow_unsaved_instance_assignment

issue_title: Error messages from ValidationError exception are not serialized correctly
Cannot use Tensor or AbstractModule as key in HashMap or HashSet
issue_body:
Currently, the `hashCode()` method in `Tensor` and `AbstractModule` is implemented using their own mutable states. 
If we put an `AbstractModule`  in a HashSet, and call forward on `AbstractModule`, then we may not find
the `AbstractModule` in the HashSet it was put in.

 For example, the following test will fail:
```scala
  "HashSet " should "work" in {
    val layer1 = Linear(4, 4)
    val hashSet = mutable.HashSet[Module[Float]]()
    hashSet.add(layer1)
    val data = Tensor[Float](4, 4).rand()
    layer1.forward(data)
    hashSet.contains(layer1) should be (true)
  }
```

issue_title: Disallow accessing internal EC2 instances which have a public IP
issue_body:
Apparently you can assign a public IP to EC2 instances in the "internal" (private) subnets. Nobody should do this, but it's technically possible.
This will allow sending data to private EC2 instances (e.g. via UDP), but the route back will not work (due to NAT).

We should configure ACLs appropriately to avoid this.

issue_title: Z3 does not manage to solve problems that require unfolding assert-based definitions occurring within the scope of quantifiers.
issue_body:
I'm embedding a modal logic into the logic of Z3 and I noticed that when I manually unfold the definitions in one of my assertions, Z3 manages to refute the clause set, while it fails if I don't unfold.

Consider the following example:

```
(set-option :produce-proofs true)
(declare-sort Prop 0)
(declare-sort W 0)

(declare-fun r (W) (List W))
(declare-fun mnot (Prop) Prop)
(declare-fun mimp (Prop Prop) Prop)
(declare-fun M (Prop) Prop)
(declare-fun T (Prop W) Bool)
(define-fun V ((p Prop)) Bool (forall ( (w W) ) (T p w)) )

(assert (forall ((w W) (p Prop)) 
  (iff (T (mnot p) w) 
       (not (T p w))   )
))

(assert (forall ((w W) (p Prop) (q Prop)) 
  (iff (T (mimp p q) w) 
       (=> (T p w) (T q w)) )
))

(define-fun-rec count ((p Prop) (l (List W)) ) Int
  (ite (= l (as nil (List W))) 0
       (ite (T p (head l)) (+ 1 (count p (tail l)))
                           (count p (tail l)) 
       )  
  )
)

(assert (forall ((w W) (p Prop)) 
  (iff
    (T (M p) w) 
    (> (count p (r w)) (count (mnot p) (r w)))
  )
))


(declare-fun senA () W)
(declare-fun senB () W)
(declare-fun senC () W)
(declare-fun s () W)

(assert (= 
  (r s)
  (insert senA (insert senB (insert senC (as nil (List W)))))
))

(declare-fun c () Prop)
(assert (T c senA))
(assert (T (mnot c) senB))
(assert (T c senC))

(assert (not 
    (T c s)
))

;;(assert (forall ((p Prop)) (V (mimp (M p) p) ) ))  ;;  [Definitions folded]

;;(assert (forall ((p Prop)) (forall ((w W)) (=> (T (M p) w) (T p w) ) ) ))  ;; [Definitions manually unfolded]

(check-sat)
(get-proof)
```

If I uncomment the last assertion (with the definitions unfolded), Z3 solves the problem. However, if I uncomment the previous assertion instead (where definitions are folded), Z3 does not terminate. It would be nice if Z3 could solve the problem in both cases.

(Note that I used `declare-fun` and `assert` to "define"  `mnot`, `mimp`, `M` and `T`, because it doesn't seem to be possible to achieve what I need with `define-fun`.  For example, to define `mnot`, with `define-fun`, the most natural way would be to turn `Prop` into an alias for the function type `W -> Bool` and then define `mnot` as a higher-order function taking a `Prop` and returning a `Prop`. As far as I know, this wouldn't be possible in the logic of Z3. Or would it?)
